6.4: shema yisrael YHWH eloheinu YHWH echad (šĕmaʿ yiśrāʾēl Yhwh ʾĕlōhênû Yhwh ʾeḥād): 'Hear O Israel: YHWH our God, YHWH is one.' The Shema is the foundational confession of Jewish faith, recited morning and evening by observant Jews. Echad (one) is the standard Hebrew numeral one — it allows for internal distinction (as in yom echad, one day, composed of evening and morning; Gen 2:24, basar echad, one flesh, composed of two persons) but asserts the unity of the divine being against all polytheism. Paul's expansion in 1 Cor 8:6 ('one God the Father ... and one Lord Jesus Christ') is not an abandonment of monotheism but a Christological reconfiguration: the Shema's single divine identity now encompasses both Father and Son.
6.1: הַמִּצְוָה הַחֻקִּים וְהַמִּשְׁפָּטִים (hammitsĕwāh haḥuqqîm wĕhammishpāṭîm) — the three categories of Deuteronomic law: the overarching commandment (mitswāh, covenant loyalty), the statutes (ḥuqqîm, ordinances without stated reason), and the rules (mishpāṭîm, case-law judgments). The singular mitswāh can function as a collective for the whole Torah or as the one great demand (love of YHWH, v5) that the rest expresses.
6.2: לְיִרְאָה (lĕyirʾāh) — "to fear." Yirʾāh (H3374) in Deuteronomy encompasses both reverential awe and practical obedience — it is not mere terror but the posture of standing before a sovereign whose word is authoritative. The goal stated here — fear → keeping → long life — traces the covenant logic: the proper orientation toward YHWH produces the behavior that results in the flourishing promised in the covenant.
6.3: The summative appeal of v3 captures the Deuteronomic pattern: shema (hear/obey) → shāmar (keep/guard) → ʿāśāh (do). The three verbs together form a comprehensive call: receive, internalize, and enact. "That it may go well with you" (ʾăšer yîṭab lāk) ties obedience to covenant blessing — not as merit but as the natural ecology of the covenant relationship.
6.5: וְאָהַבְתָּ אֵת יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ (wĕʾāhavtā ʾēt Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā) — "you shall love the LORD your God." Āhab (H157) in a covenant context denotes not merely warm feeling but the committed loyalty of a covenant partner. The love required here is structured by three terms: lĕbābĕkā (your heart — the decision-making center and will), napšĕkā (your soul/life — the whole self, including emotions and vitality), and mĕʾōdekā (your might/abundance — all your resources and energy). Jesus cites this as the greatest commandment (Matt 22:37), adding "mind" (dianoia) which renders the Deuteronomic lēb in its full cognitive dimension. Together the three/four faculties form a merism: the entirety of human personhood is to be oriented toward YHWH.
6.6: עַל לְבָבֶךָ (ʿal lĕbābĕkā) — "on your heart." The location of the commandment is the heart, not a stone tablet or scroll: internalizing precedes externalizing. This verse is the precursor to the new covenant promise of Jer 31:33 ("I will put my law on their hearts") — the Deuteronomic demand for heart-law becomes the covenantal goal that the Sinaitic system could specify but not guarantee.
6.7: וְשִׁנַּנְתָּם (wĕšinnantām) — "you shall teach them diligently/sharpen them." The verb (H8150) has the nuance of incision — sharp, repeated instruction. The contexts of teaching listed — sitting, walking, lying down, rising — are the complete round of daily life. The Torah is not to be reserved for formal instruction but woven into every conversational moment.
6.8: קְשַׁרְתָּם לְאוֹת (qĕšartām lĕʾôt) — "bind them as a sign on your hand." The command became the basis for the practice of tefillin (phylacteries) — small boxes containing Torah texts worn on the hand and forehead. Whether the original text intends the physical practice or a metaphor for constant Torah-mindedness (as Prov 3:3 uses similar language metaphorically) is debated. Jesus criticizes the Pharisaic expansion of the practice (Matt 23:5, "they make their phylacteries broad") without dismissing the underlying principle.
6.9: מְזוּזוֹת (mĕzûzôt) — "doorposts." The mezuzah practice (affixing Torah texts to doorposts) arises from this command. The doorpost was already a covenantal marker from the Passover blood ritual (Exod 12:7, 22-23). Writing Torah on the gates frames the household as a covenant space: entering and leaving, the family passes under the sign of the covenant.
6.10: The transition from wilderness to settled land poses the chief Deuteronomic spiritual danger: prosperity-induced forgetting. "Houses filled with all good things that you did not fill" — the unearned abundance of the land is YHWH's gift, not Israel's achievement. The ethical danger of inherited blessing is the delusion of self-sufficiency.
6.11: The accumulation of unearned goods — "cisterns cut out that you did not cut, vineyards and olive trees that you did not plant" — forms the material backdrop for the warning of v12. This is the classic Deuteronomic prosperity trap: the blessings of covenant faithfulness can, paradoxically, generate the forgetfulness that breaks the covenant.
6.12: פֶּן תִּשְׁכַּח (pen tishkaḥ) — "take care that you do not forget." The verb shākaḥ (H7911, to forget) is the negative counterpart to the constant Deuteronomic imperative to remember (zākar). Forgetting YHWH means losing the Exodus-identity that defines Israel as a people. Memory is not merely psychological but covenantal: to forget is to functionally deny the relationship.
6.13: אֶת יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ תִּירָא וְאֹתוֹ תַעֲבֹד (ʾet Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā tîrāʾ wĕʾōtô taʿăbōd) — "the LORD your God you shall fear, and him you shall serve." The emphatic word order places YHWH (ʾōtô) before the verbs — exclusive devotion: YHWH alone is to be feared and served. Jesus quotes this verse directly against Satan in the wilderness (Matt 4:10: "you shall worship the Lord your God, and him only shall you serve"), using proskynēseis (worship/bow down) for tîrāʾ and latreuō (serve/render religious service) for taʿăbōd.
6.14: The prohibition "do not go after other gods" (lōʾ tēlĕkûn ʾaḥărê ʾĕlōhîm ʾăḥērîm) uses the verb hālak ʾaḥărê (to walk after, to follow) — the language of personal allegiance and following a lord. Going after other gods is the covenantal equivalent of desertion.
6.15: אֵל קַנָּא — jealous God (as in 5:9). The phrase "lest the anger of the LORD your God be kindled against you and he destroy you from the face of the earth" (wĕhišmidĕkā mēʿal pĕnê hāʾădāmāh) is the ultimate covenant sanction — exclusion from covenant life and from the land that is covenant existence. The stakes of covenant unfaithfulness are not merely behavioral correction but existential annihilation of the community.
6.16: לֹא תְנַסּוּ (lōʾ tĕnassû) — "you shall not put to the test." Nāsāh (H5254) means to test or prove — to act as if YHWH's presence or power needs demonstration. "As you tested him at Massah" (Exod 17:1-7, where the people demanded water as proof of YHWH's presence) describes the paradigm: covenant testing demands proof before trusting. Jesus quotes this exact verse (Matt 4:7; Luke 4:12) against the devil's proposal to test YHWH by a staged fall requiring angelic rescue.
6.17: שָׁמוֹר תִּשְׁמְרוּן (shāmôr tišmĕrûn) — the infinitive absolute with the finite verb: "diligently keep" (emphatic/intensive). The doubled verbal construction is characteristic of Deuteronomy's rhetorical emphasis; it heightens the urgency of observance beyond routine compliance to vigilant guarding.
6.18: הַיָּשָׁר וְהַטּוֹב (hayyāshār wĕhaṭṭôb) — "the right and the good." This phrase goes beyond statutory compliance: "what is right and good in the sight of the LORD" is a moral category beyond mere legal conformity. Deuteronomy regularly supplements the legal corpus with this kind of character-orienting language, pointing to a disposition-level righteousness rather than merely a conduct-level compliance.
6.19: The goal of covenant obedience includes military and territorial consequences: thrusting out (hădōp) enemies as YHWH promised. The promise of land is covenantally conditioned — the people's fidelity is the channel through which YHWH's prior promise flows into realized inheritance.
6.20: The catechetical question — "what do these testimonies and statutes and rules mean?" — anticipates the next generation's need for covenant formation. The child's question is the occasion for the Exodus-recital (vv21-25): law is always explained by prior redemption. The statutory life of Israel is unintelligible apart from its Exodus origin. This pattern — law after narrative, command after grace — is the structure of Deuteronomy's ethics.
6.21: עֲבָדִים הָיִינוּ לְפַרְעֹה (ʿăbādîm hāyînû lĕparʿōh) — "we were slaves to Pharaoh." The first-person plural encompasses every generation: the Exodus narrative is the community's constitutive story, not just ancient history. Wayyōṣiʾēnû Yhwh ("and YHWH brought us out") — the divine act of liberation precedes and generates the legal obligations of the covenant.
6.22: The Exodus signs and wonders (ʾōtôt ûmôpĕtîm) are the empirical foundation of the covenant's credibility. YHWH is not known abstractly but through observed, narrated historical action. The "great and terrible signs" do not generate belief as proof but provide the narrative content for a faith that is already constituted by covenant relationship.
6.23: לְהָבִיא אֹתָנוּ (lĕhābîʾ ʾōtānû) — "to bring us in." The Exodus is not primarily an escape-from but a bringing-to: the goal is the land, not the wilderness. The liberation from Egypt has a telos — covenant life in the promised land — and the whole Torah is the charter for that life.
6.24: לְיִרְאָה אֶת יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ (lĕyirʾāh ʾet Yhwh ʾĕlōhênû) — "to fear the LORD our God." The purpose of the statutes is not arbitrary obedience but the formation of the fear-of-YHWH that is the beginning of wisdom (Prov 9:10). "For our good always" (lĕṭôb lānû kol-hayyāmîm) — covenant law is not an imposition but a gift; the commandments are structurally oriented toward the community's flourishing.
6.25: וּצְדָקָה תִּהְיֶה לָּנוּ (ûṣĕdāqāh tihyeh lānû) — "and it will be righteousness for us." Ṣĕdāqāh (H6666) here functions not as a forensic verdict but as a relational/ethical state: the community that keeps the covenant commandments stands in right relation (ṣedeq) with YHWH. This is not works-righteousness in a transactional sense; it is the description of what covenant faithfulness looks like from the inside. Paul's argument in Romans (esp. 10:5-6) distinguishes this righteousness of the law from the righteousness that is by faith, but both operate within the framework of covenant relation.

18.15: navi mikirbecha meacheicha kamoni yaqim lecha YHWH eloheicha elav tishmaun (nābîʾ miqqirbĕkā mēʾahêkā kāmōnî yāqîm lĕkā Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā ʾēlāw tišmāʿûn): 'A prophet like me will YHWH your God raise up for you from among your brothers; to him you shall listen.' The singular prophet (navi) can be read as: (1) a category or series of prophets who will continue Moses's role; (2) an individual eschatological figure. The Qumran community awaited a specific prophetic figure alongside the Messiah and the Aaronic priest (1QS 9:11). Peter and Stephen in Acts 3 and 7 take reading (2): the specific individual is Jesus, whose coming makes the definitive Torah-interpretation that Moses could only anticipate.
18.1: No portion or inheritance (v. 1). The Levitical priests receive no tribal territory in the land allotment. נַחֲלָה (nachalah) means “inheritance, share” — the same word used of each tribe’s land grant. The Levites’ exclusion from land is a positive statement: YHWH himself replaces the land. אִשֵׁי יְהוָה (ishei YHWH) = “YHWH’s fire-offerings” — the altar portions — constitute their material income. The MKT’s “fire-offerings of the LORD” preserves the liturgical specificity over generic “the LORD’s offerings.”
18.2: YHWH is their inheritance (v. 2). The statement is direct and personal: יְהוָה הוּא נַחֲלָתוֹ — “YHWH, he is his inheritance.” The personal pronoun hu’ lends emphasis. This exact formulation recurs in Ps 16:5, where the psalmist claims YHWH as his portion — a verse Peter applies to Christ’s resurrection (Acts 2:25-28). The priestly pattern anticipates the NT reversal where all believers are “royal priests” who inherit YHWH himself (1 Pet 2:9).
18.3: Priestly portions (v. 3). The three prescribed portions — זְרֹעַ (zero’a, arm/shoulder), לְחָיַיִם (lechayyim, cheeks/jaw), קֵבָה (qevah, stomach/maw) — specify which parts of the sacrificial animal belong to the officiating priest. The Mishnah (Chullin 10) debates these boundaries in detail. The MKT “shoulder, cheeks, and stomach” follows the standard rendering; “maw” (KJV) is archaic English for the stomach of a ruminant.
18.4: Firstfruits (v. 4). רֵאשִׁית (reshit) = “firstfruits, first/beginning” — the same word in Gen 1:1 (bereshit). The offering list — grain, wine, oil, first fleece — parallels the harvest calendar festivals. גֵז (gez, first shearing) appears only here and Job 31:20 in this agricultural sense. Giving the first fleece to the priest acknowledges YHWH as the source of the flock’s fertility.
18.5: Chosen to minister in the name of YHWH (v. 5). Three key terms converge: בָחַר (bachar, chosen) — the election language of Deut 7:6 applied now to the priestly tribe; שָׁרַת (sharat, minister/serve in the sanctuary) — distinguished from avad (ordinary work); and בְשֵׁם יְהוָה (beshem YHWH, in the name of YHWH) — to act in the name is to act with the authority and identity of the one named. The phrase recurs in vv. 7, 22 and is foundational for NT “in the name of Jesus” usage.
18.6: A Levite from any town (v. 6). Any Levite scattered in the towns may present himself and serve, driven by his נֶפשׁ (nefesh): “with all his desire/self.” Nefesh here carries the sense of personal longing — the whole living person’s orientation — not a disembodied soul. The MKT’s “whenever he wishes” conveys the voluntary, desire-driven character of the move. YHWH chose them (bachar), but individual Levites exercise their own will in presenting for service.
18.7: Equal standing (v. 7). The Levite who comes from the towns “ministers in the name of YHWH his God, just as all his fellow Levites who stand before the LORD there do.” עָמַד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה = “stand before YHWH” — a technical term for priestly service in the sanctuary presence. All Levites share the same standing regardless of origin; there is no priestly aristocracy within the tribe based on city of origin.
18.8: Equal shares (v. 8). The incoming Levite receives equal food portions alongside the resident Levites. The phrase לְבַד מִמְכָרָיו עַל-הָאָבוֹת is contested: “besides what he has from the sale of his patrimony” (MKT), or perhaps “besides his inherited shares.” The principle is clear: no financial disadvantage for choosing full-time sanctuary service.
18.9: Detestable practices of the nations (v. 9). תּוֹעֲבֹת הַגּוֹיִם (to’avot haggoyim, detestable practices of the nations) — to’evah (abomination/detestable thing) is the strongest Hebrew term of moral revulsion. The prohibition triggers the list in vv. 10-12. Israel’s distinctive identity as YHWH’s people is maintained by categorical separation from the surrounding cultures’ methods of accessing the divine or the future.
18.10: Occult practices list I (v. 10). The first group: מַעֲבִיר בְנוֹ-בִתּוֹ בָאֵשׁ (passes son/daughter through fire — child sacrifice); קֹסֵם קְסָמִים (qosem qesamim, practices divination); מְעוֹנֵן (augur/interprets omens); מְנַחֵשׁ (menachesh, divines — from nachash, serpent; observes omens). These represent attempts to manipulate or know the future apart from YHWH’s revealed word.
18.11: Occult practices list II (v. 11). מְכַשֵׁף (mekashef, sorcerer — the Septuagint renders this pharmakos, root of “pharmacy” in the sense of magical potions); חֹבֵר חָבֶר (casts spells/binds — binding incantations); שֹאֵל אוֹב (consults a medium — ov, a spirit of the dead; the Witch of Endor uses this in 1 Sam 28); יִדְּעֹנִי (spiritist/wizard — “knowing one”); דֹרֵשׁ אֶל-הַמֵּתִים (necromancer — seeks the dead). The range spans all forms of unauthorized access to hidden knowledge.
18.12: Detestable to YHWH (v. 12). Every person practicing these things is תּוֹעֲבַת יְהוָה (an abomination to YHWH) — the personal construction (not just “this practice” but “this person”). This is why YHWH drives out the nations. The expulsion from Canaan is explicitly framed as moral judgment, not ethnic preference. Israel is the instrument of YHWH’s justice against generations of occult practice.
18.13: Blameless before YHWH (v. 13). תָמִים (tamim, blameless/whole/complete) — the same word describing Noah (“blameless in his generation,” Gen 6:9) and the unblemished sacrificial animal. It connotes integrity, completeness, undivided loyalty. Contrasted with the occult practitioners in vv. 10-12, Israel’s blamelessness means exclusive reliance on YHWH’s revelation rather than unauthorized divination.
18.14: You are different (v. 14). The nations being dispossessed “heed omen-readers and diviners.” וְאַתָּה לֹא-כֵן (but as for you, not so) — emphatic contrast. YHWH does not leave Israel without access to divine guidance; he provides the prophetic office as his chosen channel, replacing all occult alternatives.
18.16: The Horeb pattern (v. 16). YHWH’s provision of a prophet is grounded in Israel’s own Horeb request — the people could not bear direct divine speech. יוֹם הַקָּהָל (yom haqahal, the day of the assembly) refers to Sinai/Horeb in Deut 9:10 and 10:4 — a fixed term for the covenant-founding assembly. The pattern is: YHWH speaks → mediator hears → Israel receives through the mediator. The prophetic office formally extends and institutionalizes what Moses exemplified at Sinai.
18.17: YHWH affirms (v. 17). YHWH’s direct speech to Moses: “What they have said is right/good” (הֵיטִיבוּ, they have done well). Israel’s request for mediation was not a failure of faith but an appropriate recognition of human limits before divine holiness. The prophet-as-mediator structure is thus divinely instituted — a pedagogical structure for covenant life before the coming of the one who would fulfill it directly.
18.18: YHWH’s words in the prophet’s mouth (v. 18). וְנָתַתִּי דְבָרַי בְפִיוֹ — “I will put my words in his mouth.” This verbal formula recurs in Jer 1:9 (YHWH’s call of Jeremiah) and Ezek 3:3 (Ezekiel swallowing the scroll). The prophet is not a mere oracle-interpreter but a mouthpiece for YHWH’s own speech. The chain of authority: YHWH → prophet → people, parallel to Moses at Sinai.
18.19: Accountability for non-compliance (v. 19). דָרַשׁ מֵעִמּוֹ (I will require/seek from him) — YHWH personally holds accountable anyone who does not heed the prophet’s words spoken in YHWH’s name. The construction is legal: to “require” in a juridical sense. Acts 3:23 quotes this verse (“everyone who does not listen to that prophet shall be destroyed”) applying it to the warning about rejecting Jesus.
18.20: Two kinds of false prophet (v. 20). The death penalty applies to: (1) הַנָּבִיא אֲשֶׁר יָזִיד לְדַבֵּר (the prophet who presumes to speak) — zud means to act arrogantly/presumptuously (same root as in 1:43, Israel’s presumptuous ascent against the Amorites); (2) speaking in the name of other gods. Both categories corrupt Israel’s relationship with YHWH at its root and carry capital penalty.
18.21: Testing a prophet (v. 21). אֵיכָה נֵדַע (how shall we know?) — יָדַע (yada, know/discern — disp=2: “know” vs. “recognize/discern”). The question is pastorally urgent: Israel’s covenant life depends on heeding true prophets and rejecting false ones, yet both claim YHWH’s authority. The test provided in v. 22 is empirical: predictive accuracy.
18.22: The empirical test (v. 22). If the predicted word does not happen (לֹא יִהְיֶה וְלֹא יָבֹא, shall not happen and shall not come), the prophet has spoken בְזָדוֹן (presumptuously/arrogantly). לֹא תָגוּר מִמֶּנּוּ (do not fear him) — false prophets may threaten, but their predictive failure exposes them. Jeremiah’s clash with Hananiah (Jer 28) is the canonical application: Hananiah prophesied peace within two years; his death within that year vindicated Jeremiah.

30.15: reeh natati lefanecha hayom et-hahayyim veet-hatov veet-hamot veet-hara (rĕʾēh nātattî lĕpānêkā hayyôm ʾet-hahayyîm wĕʾet-haṭṭôb wĕʾet-hammāwet wĕʾet-hārāʿ): 'See I have set before you today life and good, and death and evil.' The covenant's binary choice — life or death, blessing or curse — is Israel's definitive moral situation. Paul's Christological reading of Deut 30 in Romans 10:6-8 is one of his most daring hermeneutical moves: the Torah's own accessibility-language ('not up in heaven, not across the sea, but very near you') is applied to the word of Christ — the gospel is the Torah's own principle of accessibility now embodied in the proclaimed word of faith.
30.1: The conditional opener — כִּי יָבֹאוּ עָלֶיךָ כָּל הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה (kî yābōʾû ʿālêkā kol-haddĕbārîm hāʾēlleh) — "when all these things come upon you, the blessing and the curse" — assumes that both outcomes will be experienced. The construction is not a simple conditional ("if") but anticipatory ("when"). Moses does not present the curse as merely hypothetical; he projects a future that includes exile. This verse opens the covenant's most forward-looking section: what happens after catastrophe.
30.2: וְשַׁבְתָּ (wĕshavtā) — "and you return": the verb shûb (H7725) is Deuteronomy's signature term for the covenant reversal that begins with the will. The phrase bĕkol lĕbābĕkā ûbĕkol napšĕkā (with all your heart and all your soul) echoes the Shema's demand-language (6:5), indicating that the return demanded is total reorientation, not grudging compliance.
30.3: וְשָׁב יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ (wĕshāb Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā) — "and YHWH your God will return." The same verb shûb used of Israel in v2 is now used of YHWH — the divine turning mirrors and responds to the human turning. This verbal mirroring is theologically significant: YHWH's restoration is not independent of Israel's repentance but neither is it merely reactive; it is the covenantal counterpart to Israel's return. Wĕriḥămĕkā ("and will have compassion on you") — the noun raḥămîm (H7356, from the root for "womb") indicates the most intimate form of divine tenderness.
30.4: נִדַּח (niddaḥ, Niphal of H5080) — "driven out/banished." The passive form indicates that the scattering is not self-chosen exile but forced expulsion. The extremity of "the ends of heaven" (qĕṣēh hashshāmāyim) marks the reach of the divine retrieval: no geographic distance places the exiled Israelite beyond the covenant's reach. Wĕmishshām — "and from there YHWH your God will gather you."
30.5: הֵיטִיב (hêṭîb, Hiphil of H3190) — "to do good to, benefit": the restoration covenant promises material improvement. The phrase mēʾăbōtêkā ("more than your fathers") introduces a superlative: the restored community will exceed in blessing what any previous generation experienced. This "better than before" motif connects forward to the new covenant vision of Jer 31 and Ezek 36.
30.6: וּמָל יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ אֶת לְבָבְךָ (ûmāl Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā ʾet lĕbābĕkā) — "and YHWH your God will circumcise your heart." The verb mûl (H4135) is the standard term for physical circumcision; applied to the heart, it describes the removal of the hardened outer covering that prevents full covenant loyalty. The decisive point is the subject: YHWH acts unilaterally. Unlike 10:16 where Israel is commanded "circumcise your heart," here YHWH performs the act himself. This is the closest Deuteronomy comes to announcing the new covenant — the heart-transformation that Jer 31:33 ("I will put my law on their heart") and Ezek 36:26 ("I will give you a new heart") will develop. Paul's argument in Rom 2:29 ("circumcision is a matter of the heart, by the Spirit") and Phil 3:3 draws directly on this trajectory.
30.7: נָתַן (nātan) — "will place/put": YHWH will place (yittēn) the curses upon the enemies. The covenant reversal is also judicial: those who afflicted Israel under divine sanction will themselves become the objects of covenant curse. This is the juridical dimension of restoration — not merely rescue but vindication.
30.8: וְאַתָּה תָשׁוּב (wĕʾattāh tāshûb) — "and you will return": the initial condition of v2 is now restated as a future certainty, not a hypothesis. The shift from conditional to indicative reflects the structure of grace: YHWH's prior action (v6, circumcision of heart) makes the human return possible and certain. Shāmaʿtā bĕqôl Yhwh — "you will obey the voice of YHWH" — the obedience follows from the heart-transformation, not the reverse.
30.9: יוֹתִירְךָ (yôtîrĕkā, Hiphil of H3498) — "will make you overflow/abound": the prosperity language echoes the blessing catalog of ch 28, but now in the register of restoration rather than initial covenant-entry. The three spheres of blessing — womb, animal stock, ground — are the fundamental productive capacities of agrarian life. Kî yāshûb Yhwh lāśôś ʿālêkā lĕṭôb kaʾăsher śās ʿal ʾăbōtêkā: "as he rejoiced over your fathers" — YHWH rejoices (śôś) over the restored people, not merely restoring a legal status but expressing divine delight.
30.10: בְּשׁוּבְךָ (bĕshûbĕkā) — "when you return": the absolute form of the condition — with all your heart (lēbāb) and all your soul (nepeš) — recapitulates the Shema's demand. The restoration covenant is not a lowering of the standard; it is the same total-person covenant loyalty, now made possible by the circumcision of v6. Bĕkol lĕbābĕkā ûbĕkol napšĕkā here serves as the closing bracket of the section that opened at v2 with the same phrase.
30.11: לֹא נִפְלֵאת (lōʾ niphlĕʾt, Niphal of H6381) — "not too wonderful/extraordinary." The root plʾ means extraordinary, beyond ordinary capacity — used of divine wonders (Exod 3:20) and things too great for human comprehension. The denial — "this commandment is not too extraordinary for you, not too far away" — counters the defeatist assumption that covenant faithfulness requires superhuman capacity. The NT picks this up; Paul quotes vv12-14 in Rom 10:6-8 to argue that Christ is not inaccessibly remote.
30.12: לֹא בַשָּׁמַיִם הִוא (lōʾ bāshāmayim hîʾ) — "not in heaven is it." The rhetorical question "who will go up to heaven for us and get it for us?" frames the alternative as an impossibility — no one is capable of the ascent required. In its Deuteronomic context, this means: the Torah does not require superhuman effort; it has been given accessibly. Paul re-reads the rhetorical question in Rom 10:6-8: "who will ascend into heaven?" (= to bring Christ down) and "who will descend into the abyss?" (= to bring Christ up from the dead) — he applies the Deuteronomic impossibility-argument typologically to show that faith does not require impossible acts because Christ has already accomplished the ascent and descent.
30.13: מֵעֵבֶר לַיָּם (mēʿēber layyām) — "beyond the sea." The paired impossibilities — heaven (v12) and sea (v13) — represent the two axes of inaccessibility in ancient cosmology: vertical and horizontal. The sea (yām) often carries the connotation of the deep and chaotic in Hebrew cosmology. Paul reads this as "the abyss" (ābyssos) in his re-application (Rom 10:7). The point in both contexts is the same: the saving word is not inaccessible by cosmic distance.
30.14: כִּי קָרוֹב אֵלֶיךָ הַדָּבָר מְאֹד (kî qārôb ʾēlêkā haddābār mĕʾōd) — "but the word is very near to you." Qārôb (H7138, near, close) is the climactic reversal of the two impossibility-scenarios. The three locations — mouth (bĕpîkā), heart (bĕlibĕkā), and the stated purpose "to do it" (laʿăśôtô) — describe a word that has already been internalized and is ready for enactment. The triad implies: oral recitation (in your mouth), inner comprehension (in your heart), and active obedience (to do it). Paul quotes this verse as the starting point for his argument that "the word of faith" (the gospel) is equally accessible — the Torah's claim for the nearness of the covenant word becomes the gospel's claim for the nearness of Christ.
30.16: לְאַהֲבָה אֶת יְהוָה (lĕʾahăbāh ʾet Yhwh) — "to love YHWH": the imperative returns to the ground-level command. Obedience and love are not presented as alternatives; the verb ʾāhab in covenant context is the encompassing term for covenantal loyalty-in-action. The three-fold structure — love YHWH, walk in his ways, keep his commandments — recapitulates the Shema's demand in its fullest form. The result: blessing and multiplication in the land.
30.17: יִפְנֶה לְבָבְךָ (yipneh lĕbābĕkā) — "if your heart turns away": the verb pānāh (H6437) here has the sense of deflection — the heart that was to be wholly toward YHWH (6:5) turning to face something else. The consequence — serving other gods — follows naturally; the heart's orientation determines the object of worship. The "turning" of the heart is the internal act that precedes all external apostasy.
30.18: אָבֹד תֹּאבֵדוּן (ābōd tōʾbēdûn) — infinitive absolute intensifying the finite verb: "you will certainly/utterly perish." The intensified form marks the severity; this is not a vague threat but a covenantal certainty. Lōʾ taʾărîkûn yāmîm ʿal hāʾădāmāh — "you will not prolong your days on the land": longevity in the land, promised for obedience (5:16; 6:2), is here reversed as the sanction for covenant defection.
30.19: הַעִידֹתִי בָכֶם הַיּוֹם אֶת הַשָּׁמַיִם וְאֶת הָאָרֶץ (haʿîdōtî bākem hayyôm ʾet hashshāmayim wĕʾet hāʾāreṣ) — "I call to witness against you today heaven and earth." The formal invocation of cosmic witnesses is the standard closing of a vassal-treaty — the suzerain calls on the divine witnesses to guarantee the covenant terms. Since YHWH himself is the suzerain, the witnesses are the impersonal creation: heaven and earth. The formal verb ʿûd (H5749, Hiphil: to bear witness/testify against) gives this a legal register: Moses is making a formal treaty-deposition. Habĕḥer baḥayyîm lĕmaʿan tiḥyeh — "choose life so that you may live" — the imperative plus result clause is the clearest single statement of Deuteronomy's entire argument.
30.20: לְאַהֲבָה אֶת יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ (lĕʾahăbāh ʾet Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā) — "to love YHWH your God": the closing verse of the section recapitulates its three-term structure: love (ʾāhab), obey (shāmaʿ bĕqôlô), hold fast (dābaq bô, H1692 — to cling to, as the vine to the trellis, as husband and wife, Gen 2:24). כִּי הוּא חַיֶּיךָ (kî hûʾ ḥayyêkā) — "for he is your life": the capstone of the whole ethical argument. YHWH is not merely the source of life as a secondary cause; he is constitutively Israel's life. Obedience is not the mechanism for securing life from YHWH; it is the way of inhabiting the life that is already YHWH himself.

1.1: Devarim (words/matters) opens Deuteronomy: this is Moses's farewell address to all Israel. The book's Hebrew name Devarim (from davar — word, thing, matter) captures the speech-act character of the entire work. The MKT renders as words. Kol-Yisra'el (all Israel) signals the covenant-community address that marks Deuteronomy's rhetoric throughout.
1.2: Eleven days from Horeb to Kadesh-barnea: the editorial note underscores that what took forty years was an eleven-day journey. The gap between the possible and the actual is the space of unbelief and disobedience. Derekh har Se'ir (by way of Mount Seir) marks the southern route through Edom.
1.3: The fortieth year, eleventh month, first day: Moses's speech is precisely dated — the final days of the wilderness journey. The chronological precision of Deuteronomy signals its legal-treaty character: covenants are dated.
1.4: The notice about Sihon and Og grounds the speech in the recent victories YHWH gave Israel — the immediate experiential evidence that YHWH is fulfilling the land-promise. Sihon king of the Amorites and Og king of Bashan are the two paradigmatic defeat-narratives.
1.5: Ho'il Moshe be'er (Moses began to expound/explain) — ba'ar means to make clear, to dig out, to engrave. The MKT's expound captures the hermeneutical act: Moses is not merely repeating the law but explaining and applying it for the new generation about to enter the land. This is the Deuteronomic interpretive posture.
1.6: Rav-lakhem shevet ba'har hazeh (you have stayed long enough at this mountain). YHWH initiates the departure from Horeb — the covenant is not just received at Sinai/Horeb but must be carried and lived in the land. The rav-lakhem formula (enough for you) recurs as a divine correction throughout Deuteronomy and Numbers.
1.7: The land-boundaries enumerated — Lebanon, the Euphrates, the Arabah, the Mediterranean — are the maximal extent of the Abrahamic promise. The MKT preserves the geographic terms (Arabah, Negev, Shephelah) as proper nouns rather than translating each. The seven-people list (Amorites, Canaanites, Hittites, Perizzites, Hivites, Jebusites) reflects the standard conquest inventory.
1.8: Re'eh natati lifnekhem et-ha'aretz (see, I have given you the land). The perfect tense of divine gift: YHWH speaks of the land as already given, though Israel must still take possession. This theological certainty-before-completion is the pattern of covenant promise. The oath to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob and to their zera (seed/descendants) is the covenant continuity-thread.
1.9: Moses's self-incapacity speech (lo-uchal levaddi — I cannot carry you alone) echoes Num 11:14 and is the ground for the leadership-structure appointment. The levaddi (alone) mirrors YHWH's aloneness as Creator and sole God (32:12) — Moses confesses the limits of human singular leadership.
1.10: The stars-of-heaven comparison (kekhokhvei ha-shamayim la-rov) invokes the Abrahamic promise of Gen 15:5 and 22:17. The fulfillment of the multiplication-promise is evidence-in-hand that YHWH keeps his covenants.
1.11: YHWH Elohei avoteikhem yosef aleikhem (may YHWH add to you a thousand times over). The elef pe'amim (thousand times) is the superlative-multiplication form — the covenant-blessing expected to compound beyond counting. The MKT preserves thousand times.
1.12: Moses describes his burden as carrying Israel's torach (burden), masa (load), and riv (strife/quarrel). The three terms together describe the full weight of covenant leadership: logistics, adjudication, and conflict management.
1.13: Anashim chakhamim vnevunim vide'im (wise, understanding, and experienced men): three qualifications for leaders. Chakham (wise) is practical skill; navon (understanding/discerning) is the ability to perceive and evaluate; yado'a (knowing/experienced) is the track record. The MKT renders as wise, discerning, and knowledgeable.
1.14: The people's assent (tov-ha-davar asher-dibarta la'asot — the thing you have spoken is good to do) is their covenantal participation in the governance structure. Deuteronomy consistently emphasizes the community's responsive role alongside YHWH's commands.
1.15: The appointment of tribal leaders over thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens: the decimal-hierarchy system. The shotrim (officials/scribes) are distinct from the shoftim (judges) — administrative support for the judicial structure.
1.16: Shame'u bein acheikhem ushfattem tsedeq (hear between your brothers and judge with righteousness). Tsadeq (righteous/right) is the standard for judgment: not sentiment, not bribery, not fear of faces. The command to hear the small like the great (kaqaton kaggadol) is the legal equality principle.
1.17: Lo takiru panim ba-mishpat (you shall not show partiality in judgment). Panim (face/presence) in this idiom is the visible markers of status — the judge who responds to the face of the rich or powerful perverts justice. Ki ha-mishpat la-Elohim hu (for judgment belongs to God): the court is always YHWH's court; human judges are his deputies. This grounds judicial independence: they have nothing to fear because the actual judge is divine.
1.18: The commands given to the judges are the implementation of Mosaic instruction. The phrase va'atsaveh (and I commanded) marks this as executed, not merely intended.
1.19: The great and terrifying wilderness (ha-midbar ha-gadol v'ha-nora') is the geographic characterization of the Sinai region — the negative space between Horeb and the land. The journey through it is both historical fact and theological test.
1.20: Moses locates the people at the hill country of the Amorites (har ha-Emori) — the borderland where YHWH's gift becomes Israel's task. Asher YHWH Eloheinu noten lanu (which YHWH our God is giving us) — present participle of ongoing gift.
1.21: Re'eh natan YHWH Eloheikhah lefaneikha et ha-aretz (see, YHWH your God has given the land before you). Re'eh (see!) is Deuteronomy's characteristic call to perception and faith. Al-tira v'lo tichat (do not fear, do not be dismayed): the double prohibition of fear is the covenant-reassurance formula. Chet (dismay/terror) carries the connotation of the breaking of moral courage.
1.22: The people's request to send spies (anashim lefaneinu — men before us) originates with them, not YHWH. In Num 13:1-2 YHWH tells Moses to send spies but adds that this is at the people's urging. Deuteronomy's retelling emphasizes the human initiative that led to the catastrophe.
1.23: Moses's consent to the spy mission is described as good in his own sight (va-yitav be'einai ha-davar). The autobiographical retrospection of Deuteronomy allows Moses to record his own role in the failure.
1.24: The twelve spies went up to the hill country (ותעלו ההרה): the ascent to survey the land is the moment of decision that will determine the generation's fate.
1.25: The spies bring back pri ha-aretz (fruit of the land) in their hands — the physical evidence of the land's goodness. Va-yomeru tovah ha-aretz asher YHWH Eloheinu noten lanu (it is a good land that YHWH our God is giving us): the spies' report is accurate about the land's quality. The problem was not the land but the giants.
1.26: Lo avitem la'alot va-tamru et-pi YHWH Eloheikhem (you were not willing to go up and you rebelled against the mouth/command of YHWH). Marah (rebelled/disobeyed) is the bitterness-rebellion root — the same root as Miriam/Marah (bitter water, Exod 15:23). The rebellion is against the pi (mouth) of YHWH — his spoken command.
1.27: The murmuring in the tents (va-taragenu be'oholikhem) is the private distrust that the spies' fear amplified. The accusation that YHWH brought them out of Egypt to deliver us into the hand of the Amorites is the covenant-inversion fear: reading YHWH's redemption as deception.
1.28: The anashim acheinu (our brothers, the scouts) reported am gadol v'ram mimenu (a people greater and taller than us). The Anakim are the giant-descended peoples of Canaan. The walled cities bashot bashamayim (fortified up to heaven) is the hyperbolic fear-language of the defeated spy report.
1.29: Moses's counter to the fear: lo ta'artsu v'lo tira'u mehen (do not be terrified, do not be afraid of them). The repetition of anti-fear commands throughout the chapter is the theological response to the psychological defeat.
1.30: YHWH Eloheikhem ha-holeikh lifneikhem hu yilachem lakhem (YHWH your God who goes before you — he will fight for you). The divine-warrior promise: YHWH as the advance guard. Ke-khol asher asah itekhem be-Mitsrayim le-eineikhem (just as he did for you in Egypt before your eyes) — experiential evidence grounds the command to trust.
1.31: Uva-midbar asher ra'ita asher nasa'akha YHWH Eloheikhah ka'asher yisa-ish et-beno (in the wilderness you saw how YHWH your God carried you as a man carries his son). The father-son metaphor for YHWH's care in the wilderness is the theological heart of this verse — the daily providential carrying that the next generation experienced. This nasa (carry/bear) prefigures the Servant who bears Israel's burdens (Isa 53:4).
1.32: Uva-davar hazeh einekhem ma'aminim ba-YHWH Eloheikhem (yet in this matter you did not trust YHWH your God). Ma'aminim (trust/believe) is the aman root — the same root as amen. The failure to aman (trust, be firm/stable in) YHWH is the definition of the wilderness generation's sin. The word that means certainty/reliability is also the word for faith.
1.33: YHWH went before them — in fire by night and cloud by day (ba-esh laila uvhe'anan yomam) — to show them the way (la-tur lahem ha-derekh). The theophanic presence as navigation-guide is Israel's most concrete wilderness experience. The fire-and-cloud is the visible covenant presence.
1.34: YHWH's anger against Israel's failure to trust: va-yishma YHWH et-qol divreikhem va-yiqtsof (YHWH heard the voice of your words and was angry). Qatsaf (be angry, wrathful) — covenant anger at covenant violation. The judgment that follows is the consequence of the heard-voice of murmuring.
1.35: The oath-based judgment: not one of these men (anashim ha-ra'im hazeh — this evil generation) will see the good land. The oath sworn (nasati et-yadi — I lifted my hand, i.e., swore) is irrevocable covenant language.
1.36: The Caleb exception: mille' acharei YHWH (he followed YHWH wholeheartedly/fully). Male (to fill/be full) with acharei (after/behind) is the wholehearted-following idiom. Caleb's faithfulness is the positive counterpart to the generation's faithlessness — the same land the faithless could not enter, the faithful inherit.
1.37: Gam-bi hitanaf YHWH biglalekhem (YHWH was angry with me also because of you). Moses includes himself in the judgment — biglalekhem (on your account) — a statement of solidarity with the community and also an explanation of his own exclusion from the land (cf. Num 20:7-13).
1.38: Joshua is appointed Moses's successor (hu yavo'ah sham — he shall enter there). Chazzeq oto (strengthen him/encourage him) — the Mosaic commission to strengthen Joshua. The chazaq (be strong) command will be Joshua's own watchword (Josh 1:6-9).
1.39: Ve-tapkhem asher amartem labaz yihyeh (your little ones who you said would become plunder). The children who were feared-for in v 22 (tapenu — our children) are those who will enter the land. The generation that protected its children from a supposedly fatal future is excluded, while the children they feared for inherit the promise.
1.40: The command to turn back to the wilderness (penu lekhem u-se'u ha-midbar) is the reversal-direction: instead of forward into the land, backward into the desert. The Red Sea road (derekh yam-suf) is the way back toward Egypt.
1.41: The people's belated confession (chatanu la-YHWH — we have sinned against YHWH) and their own initiative to go up and fight is the wrong response to judgment: they confess sin but do not repent — they act on their own strength rather than YHWH's command. Va-takhintu (you girded yourself) is the language of self-equipped military readiness without divine authorization.
1.42: YHWH's prohibition: lo ta'alu v'lo tilachamu (do not go up and do not fight) — the prohibition comes after the unauthorized decision, not before. The covenant-pattern: YHWH commands, Israel rebels, Israel suffers, Israel tries to self-correct without repentance, YHWH withholds help.
1.43: Va-temartem et-pi YHWH va-tazidu (you rebelled against YHWH's command and acted presumptuously). Zud/zid (act presumptuously/arrogantly) is the key term for willful sin — acting without authorization, overstepping the covenant boundary. The MKT renders as acted presumptuously.
1.44: The Amorites defeated them at Seir (ka-asher ta'asnah ha-dvorim — as bees do). The bee-swarm metaphor for the enemy attackers is vivid: numerically overwhelming, painful, unstoppable without YHWH's protective intervention. The defeat at Hormah (va-yakkitu etkhem be-Se'ir ad-Chormah) is the historical confirmation of YHWH's withheld help.
1.45: Israel's weeping (va-tashvu va-tivku lifnei YHWH) receives no response: YHWH did not hear their voice (v'lo shama YHWH b'qolkhem). The deaf ear of YHWH to willful sinners who cry only after judgment has fallen — not repentance but the pain of consequences — is the sober covenant warning.
1.46: Va-teshvu b'Qadesh yamim rabbim ka-yamim asher yeshavtem (you stayed at Kadesh many days, all the days that you stayed there). The elliptical ending — the summarizing all the days you stayed there — is the temporal summary of the wilderness waiting before the journey resumes. The staying is the result of the staying-too-long at Horeb (v 6) taken to its ironic extreme: Israel could have been in the land; instead they stayed in the wilderness for thirty-eight more years.

2.1: The turning back to the wilderness toward the Red Sea (penu va-se'u ha-midbar derekh yam-suf) is the forty-year detour reduced to a phrase. The long time in Seir (yemim rabbim) encompasses thirty-eight years of wilderness wandering.
2.2: YHWH speaks to Moses: the resumption of divine speech after the judgment of ch 1 is the covenant-continuation. YHWH does not permanently withhold his word from Moses despite the people's failure.
2.3: Rav lakhem sov et ha-har hazeh (you have been going around this mountain long enough). The second rav lakhem (enough for you) of Deuteronomy — the first was at Horeb (1:6), now at Seir. The pattern: YHWH marks the end of each waiting-period and initiates movement.
2.4: The Esau-people (am Esau) are Israel's brothers (acheikhem) — the Jacob-Esau family relationship is the theological ground for non-aggression toward Edom. The command hishashru me'od (be very careful) adds intensity: the care required is maximal.
2.5: Lo eten lakhem me'artsam ad midhrak kaf-ragel (I will not give you any of their land — not even a footstep). The specificity of the non-grant is deliberate: just as YHWH has given Seir to Esau as a possession (yerushah), so YHWH has given Israel its inheritance separately. The inheritance-theology of Deuteronomy applies even to non-covenant nations.
2.6: The purchase of food and water from Edom (kesep tishberu) rather than taking it is the practical application of the non-aggression command. Israel is to be a paying guest through Edomite territory.
2.7: Ki YHWH Eloheikhah berakhakha b'khol ma'aseh yadekha (YHWH your God has blessed you in all the work of your hands). The blessing in ordinary labor (ma'aseh yadekha — the work of your hands) is the covenant-provision theology: the daily sustenance of the wilderness journey is the outcome of divine blessing, not coincidence. Yada YHWH (YHWH knew/watched over) — the intimate yada (know) applied to YHWH's oversight of their forty-year journey.
2.8: The passage through Edom (va-na'avor me'et acheinu venei Esav) without conflict is the demonstration of YHWH's ordering of geopolitical relationships. Elath and Ezion-geber are the port cities at the northern end of the Gulf of Aqaba — the route through Edom's territory.
2.9: The command not to harass Moab (al-tsar et-Mo'av v'al-titgar bam milchamah) mirrors the Edom prohibition. The Lot-connection: uvnei Lot natati et-Ar yerushah (I have given Ar to the sons of Lot as a possession). The descendants of Abraham's nephew receive their territory by divine grant, just as Israel does.
2.10: The antiquarian note about the Emim (giants, am gadol v'rav v'ram ka-Anakim — a great, numerous, and tall people like the Anakim) explains the pre-existing population of Moabite territory. The editorial notes in ch 2 (vv 10-12, 20-23) are parenthetical historical insertions typical of Deuteronomic narrative style.
2.11: The Emim were counted as Repha'im (the giant-descended peoples). The Moabites called them Emim; the Israelites recognized them as Rephaim. The terminological note shows the narrator's interest in the prehistory of the nations surrounding Israel.
2.12: The Horites (ha-Chorim) previously inhabited Seir before Esau's descendants dispossessed them. The comparison to Israel dispossessing Canaan (ka-asher asah Yisra'el le-erets yerushato) provides a typological parallel: just as Esau received his territory through conquest, so will Israel.
2.13: The crossing of the Zered brook (nachal Zered) marks the end of the Edom/Seir detour and the beginning of the approach to Moab. Qumu v'ivru (rise up and cross) — YHWH's command initiates each movement.
2.14: The thirty-eight-year duration (shloshim u'shmoneh shanah) from Kadesh-barnea to the Zered crossing is the precise measurement of the judgment-period — the time from the spy-rebellion until the entire military generation died. The note that YHWH swore against them (asher nishba YHWH lahem) connects this to the oath of 1:34-35.
2.15: Ki af yad-YHWH hayetah bam (for indeed the hand of YHWH was against them). The divine active opposition to the oath-violating generation: YHWH's yad (hand) is elsewhere the instrument of salvation (1:30); here it is the instrument of judgment. The hand that carries (1:31) also strikes when covenant is violated.
2.16: The all-men-of-war having perished (va-yehi ka-asher tamu kol-anshei ha-milchamah lamut) is the condition that makes the forward movement possible. The judgment was complete; the new generation can now proceed.
2.17: YHWH speaks to Moses again: the renewed divine speech after the completion of judgment signals the covenant resumption. The pattern of Deuteronomy's narrative is death-judgment followed by YHWH's re-engagement with the surviving community.
2.18: Attah over et-gevul Mo'av et-Ar (today you are crossing the border of Moab, through Ar). The day-of-crossing precision connects to the calendrical exactness of ch 1 — covenant history is precisely dated.
2.19: The command not to harass Ammon (al-tsar et-benei Ammon v'al-titgar bam) extends the non-aggression principle to the third Abrahamic-adjacent people. U-venei Lot netatiha yerushah (I have given it as a possession to the sons of Lot) — the same inheritance-theology as v 9.
2.20: The land of Ammon was also counted as Rephaim-territory (erets Repha'im techashev af hi). The Ammonites called the former inhabitants Zamzummim (another name for the giant-descended Rephaim).
2.21: The Zamzummim were as great and numerous and tall as the Anakim, but YHWH destroyed them before Ammon and Ammon possessed their territory. The divine-sovereign destruction of pre-existing populations is the theological pattern that anticipates Israel's own conquest.
2.22: The comparison: as YHWH did for Esau's descendants in Seir (destroying the Horites) and for the Moabites (destroying the Emim), so he will do for Israel. The pattern of YHWH clearing territory for the nations he assigns it to establishes his universal sovereign claim over all land.
2.23: The Avvim (ha-Avvim) who lived in villages as far as Gaza were destroyed by the Caphtorites (likely the Sea Peoples/Philistines) who came from Caphtor. The note extends the pattern of divinely-governed territorial change beyond Israel's direct experience.
2.24: Qumu se'u u-ivru et-nachal Arnon (rise up, journey, and cross the Arnon). The Arnon crossing marks the entry into territory that YHWH has given Israel to conquer. Natatati v'yadkha et-Sichon (I have given Sichon into your hand) — the past-tense divine gift of a future conquest: prophetic certainty expressed as already-accomplished.
2.25: Ha-yom hazeh achel tet pachd'kha v'yir'atka al-penei ha-ammim tachat kol ha-shamayim (this day I will begin to put the dread of you and fear of you on all peoples under all the heavens). The divine spreading of Israel's pachad (dread/terror) and yir'ah (fear) on the nations is the reputation-effect of YHWH's victories. The chalchala (writhing/anguish) of the nations at the report of Israel is the physical symptom of this divinely-induced terror.
2.26: Moses sent messengers (mal'akhim) from the wilderness of Kedemoth with words of peace (divrei shalom) to Sichon. Even to the enemy Israel must offer peace before engaging in battle — the Deuteronomic war law (20:10-12) is applied before it is formally stated.
2.27: The request to pass through on the king's highway (ba-derekh ba-derekh elek — on the road, on the road I will travel) without turning right or left (lo asur yamin u'smol) is the standard non-aggression travel-request. The same request was made to Edom (Num 20:17-18).
2.28: The offer to buy food and water (okhel tishbor li b'khesep) mirrors the Edom arrangement (v 6). Israel offers full commercial exchange, not conquest.
2.29: The reference to the sons of Esau and Moab having allowed Israel to pass through (ka-asher asu li venei Esav ha-yoshevim b'Se'ir v'ha-Mo'avim ha-yoshevim b'Ar) establishes the precedent. Until crossing the Jordan, Israel's policy is peaceful transit.
2.30: Va-lo avah Sichon melekh Cheshbon ha'avireinu bo (but Sichon king of Heshbon was not willing to let us pass through him). Ki hiqshah YHWH Eloheikhah et-rucho v'imets et-levavo (for YHWH your God hardened his spirit and made his heart courageous/stubborn). The hardening of Sichon's spirit by YHWH is the Pharaoh-pattern (Exod 4:21, 7:3) applied to the Amorite king: YHWH uses Sichon's refusal to create the occasion for Israel's victory and the beginning of conquest.
2.31: Ra'eh hachiloti tet lefaneikha et-Sichon (see, I have begun to give Sichon before you). The hachiloti (I have begun) marks the incipient conquest — the first of many such divine deliveries. Hachel rash le-rish et-artso (begin to take possession of his land) — the divine initiative empowers the human action.
2.32: Sichon came out against Israel at Jahaz (va-yetse Sichon liqratenu hu v'khal-ammo) — the initiative for battle was Sichon's. Israel did not attack without provocation; Sichon refused passage and then attacked. The va-yitten YHWH Eloheinu otho lefaneinu (YHWH our God gave him into our hand) is the covenant-victory formula.
2.33: Israel struck him and his sons and all his people (va-nakh otho v'et-banav v'et-khal-ammo). The cherem-logic (though the term is not used here explicitly) applies: total defeat of the enemy as YHWH's military-holy act.
2.34: Va-nilkhod et-khal-arav ba-et ha-hi (we captured all his cities at that time). The ba-et ha-hi (at that time) anchors the summary in the specific historical moment. The nishim v'taf (men, women, and children) all under the ban (nakhrim) — the total-devotion formula of holy war.
2.35: Only the livestock and plunder of the captured cities were kept by Israel — the standard exception to the ban: animals and goods are taken as spoil; persons are not.
2.36: From Aroer on the Arnon to Gilead: no city was too strong (lo hayetah qiryah asher sagvah mimmennu) for YHWH's giving. The comprehensive character of the victory is attributed entirely to YHWH's sovereign gift.
2.37: The exception: raq el-erets benei Ammon lo qaravta (but to the land of the Ammonites you did not go near). The specific prohibition against Ammonite territory (v 19) was observed: YHWH's non-conquest-zones were honored. This demonstrates that Israel's obedience in the Transjordan was genuine — they took exactly what YHWH gave and left what YHWH excluded.

3.11: ki raq-Og melekh-haBashan nish'ar miyeter haRefa'im hineh arso arso-barzel (kî raq-ʿÔg melek-habbāšān nišʾar miyyeter hārĕpāʾîm hinnēh ʿarśô ʿereś barzel): 'For only Og the king of Bashan was left of the remnant of the Rephaim. Behold, his bed was a bed of iron.' The Rĕpāʾîm are a category of pre-Israelite inhabitants of Canaan associated with unusual size and formidable strength (Gen 14:5; 15:20; Deut 2:10-11, 20-21). Og's iron bed (ʿereś barzel) — nine cubits long (approximately 13.5 feet) and four cubits wide — was displayed at Rabbah of the Ammonites as a trophy confirming the tradition of his extraordinary stature. The defeat of Og, like that of Sihon (ch. 2), demonstrates that YHWH's guarantee of victory over the giant clans was not empty: the most formidable pre-Israelite ruler east of the Jordan was given into Israel's hand.
3.22: lo-tira'u otam ki YHWH Eloheichem hu-hanilcham lachem (lōʾ tîrĕʾû mēhem kî Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkem hû hananicḥlām lākem): 'You shall not fear them, for it is the LORD your God who fights for you.' The divine warrior formula — YHWH himself as Israel's fighter — is the theological summary of Israel's entire military history east of the Jordan. Hu-hanilcham lachem (he is the one who fights for you): the participial form stresses YHWH's ongoing fighting posture, not a one-time act. This formula recurs throughout the conquest narrative (Josh 10:42; 23:3,10) and becomes the basis of Israel's liturgical confidence in YHWH as the warrior who goes before his people (Exod 14:14; Ps 18).
3.26: vayit'aber YHWH bi lema'ankhem velo-shama elai vayomer YHWH elai rav-lach al-tosef daber elai od badavar hazeh (wayyitʿabbēr Yhwh bî lĕmaʿankhem wĕlōʾ šāmaʿ ʾēlay wayyōʾmer Yhwh ʾēlay rab-lāk ʾal-tôsep̄ dabbēr ʾēlay ʿôd baddābār hazzeh): 'But the LORD was angry with me on your account and would not listen to me. And the LORD said to me, 'Enough from you; do not speak to me of this matter again.'' Wayyitʿabbēr YHWH bî — YHWH crossed over into anger against Moses on Israel's account (lĕmaʿankhem). The verb ʿābar in the Hithpael means to become intensely angry. Moses's intercessory prayer for permission to cross the Jordan (vv. 23-25) was denied with finality: rab-lāk (enough for you). Moses is forbidden from the land not because he is unloved (YHWH's tenderness in v. 27 is evident) but because his striking of the rock (Num 20:7-12) had disqualified him and because he bore the consequences of Israel's corporate sin as their representative leader.

4.2: lo-tosefu al-hadavar asher anochi metzaveh etchem velo tigre'u mimenu lishmor et-mitzvot YHWH Eloheichem asher anochi metzaveh etchem (lōʾ tōsipû ʿal-haddābār ʾăšer ʾānōkî mĕṣawweh ʾetkkem wĕlōʾ tigĕrĕʾû mimmennû lišmōr ʾet-miṣwōt Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkem ʾăšer ʾānōkî mĕṣawweh ʾetkkem): 'You shall not add to the word that I command you, nor take from it, that you may keep the commandments of the LORD your God that I command you.' The double prohibition — neither addition (tōsipû) nor subtraction (tigĕrĕʾû) — defines the canonical boundary of Torah. The same formula reappears in Deut 12:32 and in Revelation's closing warning (22:18-19), which applies it to the entirety of Scripture. The principle is that the covenant document is complete and authoritative as given; human modification in either direction distorts the covenant relationship it mediates.
4.12: vayedabber YHWH aleichem mitoch ha'esh kol devarim atem shomim utemunah einekhem ro'im zulati kol (waydabbēr Yhwh ʾălêkem mittôk hāʾēš qôl dĕbārîm ʾattem šōmĕʿîm ûtĕmûnāh ʾênkem rōʾîm zûlātî qôl): 'Then the LORD spoke to you out of the midst of the fire. You heard the sound of words, but saw no form; there was only a voice.' The programmatic invisibility of YHWH at Horeb is Deuteronomy's key theological safeguard against idolatry: Israel heard kol devarim (sound/voice of words) but saw temunah (form, likeness) of none. The noun temunah is the same word Moses uses of himself in Num 12:8 (Moses sees the temunah of YHWH — a special prophetic privilege that proves the rule of hiddenness). The invisibility grounds the second commandment: since YHWH showed no visible form, any representation is a falsification. John 1:18 ('no one has ever seen God') and John 5:37 ('his form you have never seen') stand in direct continuity with this Deuteronomic tradition.
4.24: ki YHWH Eloheicha esh okhlah hu El kanna (kî Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā ʾēš ʾōklāh hûʾ ʾēl qannāʾ): 'For the LORD your God is a consuming fire, a jealous God.' Esh okhlah (consuming fire) — fire that devours completely, leaving nothing. The image combines theophanic fire (Horeb), divine holiness, and judicial wrath. El qanna — the root qn' (jealousy/zeal) describes YHWH's exclusive claim on Israel's allegiance, corresponding to the marriage metaphor of covenant: YHWH as Israel's husband tolerates no rivals (Exod 20:5; 34:14). Hebrews 12:29 cites this verse directly in its climactic warning: 'for our God is a consuming fire' — the God of the new covenant is no less holy, his covenant-demands no less total, than at Sinai.
4.35: Atah hor'eta lada'at ki YHWH hu ha-Elohim ein od milvado (ʾAttāh horrĕtā lādaʿat kî Yhwh hûʾ hāʾĕlōhîm ʾên ʿôd milvaddô): 'To you it was shown, that you might know that the LORD is God; there is no other besides him.' Ein od milvado — there is nothing else apart from him. This is Deuteronomy's foundational monotheistic formula, repeated in v. 39 and echoed throughout Second Isaiah (Isa 45:5,6,14,18,21,22; 46:9). The Horeb revelation — fire, darkness, cloud, and the voice of words — was given precisely to produce cognitive certainty (lādaʿat, to know) of YHWH's sole deity. The NT applies the same epistemological structure to Christ: the incarnation as the definitive showing (horrĕtā) that enables knowledge of God (John 1:18: the only God who is at the Father's side — he has made him known).

5.1: שְׁמַע יִשְׂרָאֵל (shĕmaʿ yiśrāʾēl) — "Hear, O Israel." Moses opens with the same summons that frames the great commandment (6:4) and Israel's foundational confession. Shema (H8085) is more than hearing; it implies attentive obedience — "hear and do." The call to all of Israel (kol-yiśrāʾēl) stresses the covenant community as a whole, not merely its leaders.
5.2: כָּרַת (kārat, H3772) — "cut a covenant": the standard Hebrew idiom for covenant-making, rooted in the sacrificial rite of cutting animals (Gen 15:10). The covenant was made at Horeb (the Deuteronomic name for Sinai), tying the legal content of chs. 5–26 to the historical event of the Exodus generation's encounter with YHWH.
5.3: The claim "not with our fathers but with us" resists covenant-as-inheritance in a passive sense. Every generation stands directly before YHWH as the covenant party. Anachnu (we) is emphatic: the covenant is not a legal artifact passed down but a living relation each generation must own. This logic drives Moses's renewal-preaching in Deuteronomy as a whole.
5.4: פָּנִים בְּפָנִים (pānîm bĕpānîm) — "face to face": the most direct possible mode of divine communication. The same phrase describes Moses's unique prophetic access (34:10; Num 12:8). The people's experience at Sinai/Horeb — hearing the divine voice from fire — was not mediated by human intermediary at that moment; the mediation (v5) came because of their fear afterward.
5.5: עָמַד בֵּין (ʿāmad bên) — "I stood between": Moses's intercessory position is both spatial (between the people and the divine fire) and covenantal (mediating word). The same verb root ʿmd describes the priest standing before YHWH to minister. Moses's role combines prophetic (declaring the word), priestly (standing between), and quasi-judicial (bringing YHWH's verdict to the people) functions.
5.6: The preamble to the Decalogue — "I am YHWH your God who brought you out" — precedes all commands. This is the standard suzerainty-treaty order: the great king identifies himself and cites his past benefactions before issuing stipulations. The commands that follow are responses to prior grace, not conditions for earning it. Bêt ʿăbādîm ("house of slavery") is Israel's pre-covenant identity; the commands define who the redeemed are now.
5.7: עַל פָּנַי (ʿal pānāy) — traditionally "before me" but literally "against my face" or "to my face." The idiom may mean "in my presence" (no other gods where I am present, i.e., anywhere) or "in addition to me" (no gods as rivals). The spatial sense reinforces that the prohibition is not merely theoretical monotheism but relational exclusivity: the God who redeemed Israel will not share the covenant relationship.
5.8: פֶּסֶל (pesel, H6459) — a graven/carved image; תְּמוּנָה (tĕmûnāh) — a likeness or form. The prohibition covers both making and possessing images. The scope is comprehensive: heaven above, earth beneath, waters under earth — the three tiers of the ancient cosmology. The concern is not merely idolatry toward foreign gods but any visual representation of YHWH himself (Deut 4:15-16: "you saw no form").
5.9: אֵל קַנָּא (ʾēl qannāʾ) — "a jealous God." Qannāʾ (H7067) is used exclusively of YHWH in the OT and denotes the exclusive claim of the covenant relationship — not petty envy but the righteous intolerance of covenant violation. The "visiting iniquity of fathers on children" clause reflects corporate solidarity: covenant faithfulness (or unfaithfulness) has generational consequences. The contrast in v10 — "steadfast love to thousands" — shows the asymmetry: punishment extends to 3-4 generations; ḥesed extends to thousands.
5.10: חֶסֶד (ḥesed, H2617) — steadfast love, covenantal loyalty. The contrast between the third-to-fourth generation of v9 and "thousands" here is deliberately asymmetric: divine judgment is limited and proportional; divine ḥesed is extensive and generous. The recipients are those who "love me and keep my commandments" — the covenant-loyal community. Ḥesed is not unconditional sentimentality but the faithfulness of a covenant partner who rewards the faithful.
5.11: לַשָּׁוְא (laššāwĕʾ, H7723) — "for emptiness/worthlessness/falsehood." The prohibition covers not only profanity (using YHWH's name as a curse or oath-word) but invoking the divine name for empty, false, or deceptive purposes — swearing falsely, invoking YHWH to guarantee a lie, or treating the divine name as a magical formula. Shāwĕʾ has the semantic range of "vain," "worthless," "false" — the name of the living God is not to be wielded for worthless ends.
5.12: שָׁמוֹר (shāmôr) — "observe, guard." In Exod 20:8, the command is zākor ("remember") the Sabbath. Deuteronomy substitutes "observe/guard" (shāmôr) — not a contradiction but a complementary nuance: Exodus ties Sabbath to creation-memory (God rested); Deuteronomy ties it to liberation-memory and the ethic of rest for all, including slaves (v14-15).
5.13: Six days of labor (ʿāsîtā kol-mĕlākĕtekā) are affirmed, not merely permitted: work is part of the created order and covenant faithfulness. The Sabbath is the seventh-day cessation from the six-day norm, not a replacement for it.
5.14: The Deuteronomic Sabbath extends rest explicitly to the ben-amatkā (your male slave) and bat-amatkā (female slave), and to the sojourner (gēr) within the gates. Exodus 20 lists the same beneficiaries but in a more compressed form. The humanitarian motivation — "so that your slave may rest as you do" — is characteristically Deuteronomic, grounding law in empathy rooted in Israel's own experience of servitude.
5.15: זָכַרְתָּ כִּי עֶבֶד הָיִיתָ (zākarttā kî ʿebed hāyîtā) — "remember that you were a slave." The Sabbath in Deuteronomy is grounded in the Exodus, not creation (Exod 20:11). Memory of servitude becomes the ethical foundation for extending rest to those who serve. The "mighty hand and outstretched arm" (yād ḥăzāqāh ûzĕrôaʿ nĕṭûyāh) is a fixed formulaic pair for the Exodus event — it appears frequently in Deuteronomy as the experiential basis for covenant obligation.
5.16: כַּבֵּד אֶת אָבִיךָ וְאֶת אִמֶּךָ (kabbēd ʾet-ʾābîkā wĕʾet-ʾimmekā): "Honor your father and your mother." Kabbēd (H3513, piel of כָּבַד) means to make heavy/weighty, i.e., to treat as significant and authoritative — not merely affection but social recognition of parental authority. The attached promise — "that your days may be long and that it may go well with you" — is the first commandment with a promise (Eph 6:2-3), and Paul cites it as still applicable to NT believers under a new framework.
5.17: לֹא תִרְצָח (lōʾ tirṣaḥ): The verb rāṣaḥ (H7523) is not the general term for killing (hārag or mût) but the specific term for unlawful homicide — murder, manslaughter requiring legal process (Num 35:16-28). It does not prohibit all killing (capital punishment and holy war are commanded elsewhere); it prohibits the taking of human life outside the covenant community's authorized frameworks.
5.18: לֹא תִנְאָף (lōʾ tinʾāp): nāʾap (H5003) — sexual betrayal of the covenant. The sexual act here is covenantally framed: marriage is the covenant between husband and wife; adultery violates both the human covenant and Israel's self-understanding as YHWH's covenant-spouse. The prophets use adultery as the primary image for Israel's idolatry (Hosea, Ezekiel) — the two transgressions share the same covenantal structure.
5.19: לֹא תִגְנֹב (lōʾ tignōb): gānab (H1589) — to steal, but also to deceive or act covertly. Rabbinic tradition reads this commandment as prohibiting kidnapping (carrying a person off to slavery) rather than property theft, since property theft has its own specific legislation. On either reading, it protects the physical and social integrity of the covenant neighbor.
5.20: עֵד שָׁוְא (ʿēd shāwĕʾ) — "a worthless/false witness." Deuteronomy uses shāwĕʾ (worthless/false) in place of Exodus 20:16's šeqer (lying). The distinction may be slight, but shāwĕʾ adds the nuance of testimony that is empty or groundless, not just deliberately false. Legal testimony in the covenant community carries divine weight; groundless accusation corrupts justice at its foundation.
5.21: Deuteronomy uses two different verbs in v21 where Exodus 20:17 uses one. For coveting a neighbor's wife: חָמַד (ḥāmad, H2530) — to delight in, to desire with pleasure. For coveting a neighbor's house, field, etc.: תִּתְאַוֶּה (titʾawweh, from אָוָה H183) — to wish for, to crave. The choice of two different verbs may distinguish relational desire (for a person) from acquisitive desire (for property), or may simply be stylistic variation. In either case, both forms of covetousness are prohibited as violations of the neighbor's whole life and estate.
5.22: קוֹל גָּדוֹל (qôl gādôl) — "a great voice": the divine voice at Sinai/Horeb is a sustained, overwhelming auditory experience. Welōʾ yāsāp — "he did not add more," implying these ten words are the complete, sealed direct divine speech. The Decalogue is uniquely distinguished: written on stone by God, spoken directly to the whole assembly without human mediation. Everything else in the law came through Moses.
5.23: The elders and tribal heads (rāʾšê šibṭêkem) approach Moses — the leaders acting for the people as a whole. The "darkness" (ḥōšek) and "cloud" (ʿānān) are the typical theophanic veil: YHWH's presence manifests in phenomena that conceal as they reveal, protecting the people from the unmediated divine glory.
5.24: כְּבֹדוֹ וְאֶת גָּדְלוֹ (kĕbōdô wĕʾet-godlô) — "his glory and greatness." Kābôd (H3519) is the weightiness, radiance, manifest importance of YHWH's presence; gādal (H1431) is the extensiveness of his power and works. The people's admission — "we have heard his voice from the fire" — confirms the unique standing of the Sinaitic theophany in Israel's history.
5.25: The people's fear of death if they continue to hear YHWH's voice directly becomes the occasion for Mosaic mediation. "This great fire will consume us" — the divine holiness is lethal to those without adequate standing before it. This is not pusillanimity but accurate anthropological assessment: the creature cannot sustain direct confrontation with uncreated holiness without a mediating structure.
5.26: אֱלֹהִים חַיִּים (ʾĕlōhîm ḥayyîm) — "the living God": this title distinguishes YHWH from dead idols that cannot speak. The question "who among all flesh has ever heard the voice of the living God speaking from fire and lived?" marks the Sinai theophany as existentially unique. The answer is: only Israel, and they survived only through YHWH's mercy.
5.27: דַּבֵּר אַתָּה (dabbēr ʾattāh) — "you speak to us." The people's delegation of hearing to Moses is the foundation of the prophetic institution. Moses as the covenant mediator becomes the pattern for all subsequent prophets, and ultimately for the prophet-like-Moses still to come (18:15). The people's request is honored: YHWH approves it (v28) and establishes Moses as the permanent mouthpiece.
5.28: YHWH's response — "they are right in all they have spoken" — validates the people's instinct. Their fear is not faithlessness; their delegation to Moses is appropriate. The divine approval of the mediatorial structure is significant: YHWH himself institutes the pattern of indirect revelation through a human spokesperson, which becomes the constitutive structure of the prophetic office.
5.29: מִי יִתֵּן (mî yittēn) — the idiom of wishing: "O that they had such a heart as this always." The divine wish expresses genuine pathos: YHWH desires the people's sustained covenantal fidelity but recognizes they do not have the heart for it. This verse is the OT's sharpest pre-Jeremiah expression of the new covenant problem — external law without transformed heart is structurally insufficient. Jeremiah 31:33 and Ezekiel 36:26 will promise the solution: a new heart.
5.30: The command "return to your tents" disperses the assembly back to domestic life after the theophanic encounter. The juxtaposition with v31 ("but as for you, stand here with me") underscores Moses's unique station: Israel returns to ordinary life; Moses remains in the divine presence to receive the full law.
5.31: אֶת כָּל הַמִּצְוָה (ʾet kol-hammitsĕwāh) — "the whole commandment." The singular mitswāh followed by the plural ḥuqqîm ûmishpāṭîm (statutes and rules) reflects Deuteronomy's structure: the one commandment (covenant loyalty) expressed in many stipulations. The law is a unity with a variegated expression.
5.32: לֹא תְסוּרוּ יָמִין וּשְׂמֹאול (lōʾ tāsûrû yāmîn ûśmōʾl) — "do not turn aside to the right or to the left." This formula for covenant faithfulness — walking the exact path without deviation — recurs throughout Deuteronomy and the Deuteronomistic History. It becomes the criterion for evaluating the kings of Israel: did they walk in the ways of David, or did they turn aside?
5.33: The goal of covenant obedience is life: lĕmaʿan tiḥyûn ("so that you may live"). Deuteronomy is consistently shaped by the life-or-death stakes of covenant keeping. The choice will be formalized in 30:15-20 as the covenant's climax: life and good, or death and evil. Obedience is not the condition for earning life but the path within which covenant life is sustained.

7.1: The list of seven nations — Hittites, Girgashites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, Jebusites — is the standard Deuteronomic enumeration of Canaan's pre-Israelite inhabitants. The number seven suggests completeness; these are all the peoples of the promised land. Waḥărēm taḥărîm ʾōtām ("you must completely destroy them") introduces the ḥerem (H2764) principle — consecrated/devoted destruction — which is theologically and historically one of the most demanding elements of Deuteronomy.
7.2: חֵרֶם תַּחֲרִים (ḥērem taḥărîm) — the infinitive absolute intensifying: "you shall utterly devote to destruction." Ḥērem designates something removed from ordinary use and dedicated to YHWH, whether by destruction or consecration. In warfare, it means the complete removal of the enemy from the land without taking spoil, because the land itself belongs to YHWH and the inhabitants' corruption is total. "You must not make a covenant (lōʾ tikrōt lāhem bĕrît) with them" — covenant-making is the opposite of ḥērem; it would assimilate Israel to the very idolatry it must purge.
7.3: The intermarriage prohibition is grounded in v4: the danger is not ethnic contamination but theological assimilation. "They will turn your sons away from following me to serve other gods" — the household becomes the entry point for covenant unfaithfulness. The prohibition is directional: the problem is not the foreign person but the foreign worship they would introduce.
7.4: The causal chain: intermarriage → turning your sons → serving other gods → YHWH's anger → swift destruction. The speed of the sanction (mĕhēr, quickly) reflects Deuteronomy's concern that covenant apostasy does not have a long runway — once the heart has turned, the consequences follow.
7.5: The commanded destruction of Canaanite religious infrastructure — altars (mizbĕḥōtāyhem), sacred pillars (maṣṣĕbōtām), Asherah poles (ʾăshērāyw), and carved images (pĕsîlêhem) — is a systematic elimination of the material culture of Canaanite religion. These objects represent the rival system that would compete for Israel's covenantal allegiance. The positive command — "burn the carved images with fire" (v25) — makes clear that the destruction is itself a liturgical act of covenantal purification.
7.6: סְגֻלָּה (sĕgullāh, H5459) — "treasured possession." The root suggests wealth closely guarded; it appears in Mal 3:17 ("they shall be mine, says the LORD of Hosts, my special possession"), Ps 135:4, and 1 Pet 2:9 (where Peter applies it to the NT church: "a people for his own possession," laos eis peripoiēsin). Sĕgullāh is Israel's most intimate covenantal title — not merely God's people but his personally treasured possession, set apart from all peoples of the earth.
7.7: לֹא מֵרֻבְּכֶם (lōʾ mērubbĕkem) — "not because you were more numerous." The negative election theology here is rare in world religions: Israel is chosen not because of size, strength, or virtue but despite being the least of peoples (haqqāṭōn mikkol hāʿammîm). This undercuts all ethnic pride and establishes election as pure divine initiative.
7.8: מֵאַהֲבַת יְהוָה אֶתְכֶם (mēʾahăbat Yhwh ʾetkem) — "because the LORD loved you." The reason for election is YHWH's own love (ʾahăbāh, H160) — a circular, irreducible reason: YHWH loves Israel because he loves Israel (cf. Deut 10:15). The second reason — keeping the oath sworn to the fathers — grounds the love in covenant faithfulness across generations. Both reasons are internal to YHWH's character, not external to Israel's merit.
7.9: הָאֵל הַנֶּאֱמָן שֹׁמֵר הַבְּרִית וְהַחֶסֶד (hāʾēl hanneʾĕmān shōmēr habbĕrît wĕhaḥesed) — "the faithful God who keeps covenant and steadfast love." Three terms define YHWH's covenantal character: neʾĕmān (faithful, trustworthy, H539), bĕrît (covenant, H1285), ḥesed (steadfast love/loyalty, H2617). Together they describe a God whose commitment to the covenant relationship is structurally reliable — not capriciously revoked by Israel's failures but faithfully maintained. "To a thousand generations" (lĕʾelep dôr) is the eschatological horizon of covenant faithfulness.
7.10: The balancing clause: YHWH "repays those who hate him to their face" (mĕšallēm lĕśōnĕʾāyw ʾel-pānāyw lĕhaʾăbîdô). The divine faithfulness in v9 is matched by a corresponding divine righteousness — YHWH does not ignore those who hate him. The covenant has two faces: blessing for the faithful, sanction for the hostile. "He will not delay" (lōʾ yĕʾaḥēr) — the divine patience has limits with those who actively oppose the covenant.
7.11: The conclusion of the "faithful God" section (vv7-11): "therefore keep the commandments, statutes, and rules." The indicative of divine election and faithfulness (vv7-10) grounds the imperative of human obedience (v11). The logic is always this order: who YHWH is and what he has done → what Israel is to do. Command follows grace.
7.12: עֵקֶב (ʿēqeb, H6118) — "because of, as a result of." The term can mean "heel" (the track of the heel, hence the consequence of walking in a certain path). The covenant blessings follow Israel's obedience as the track follows the foot — not because obedience earns blessing but because walking the covenant path leads through covenant blessing. Shāmar (to keep) + ḥāsēd (YHWH's loyal love) — the correspondence of human keeping and divine keeping is the covenant's relational structure.
7.13: וְאֵהֵבְךָ וּבֵרַכְךָ וְהִרְבֶּךָ (wĕʾēhēbĕkā ûbēraḵĕkā wĕhirbĕkā) — "he will love you, bless you, and multiply you." Three verbs of divine action flowing from covenant faithfulness: love (prior commitment renewed in blessing), bless (material and social flourishing), multiply (generational continuity). The blessings listed — womb, ground, grain, wine, oil, herd, flock — are the fundamental fertility goods of agricultural life.
