7.14: בָּרוּךְ תִּהְיֶה מִכָּל הָעַמִּים (bārûk tihyeh mikkol hāʿammîm) — "blessed more than all peoples." The comparative "more than" (mikkol) situates Israel's covenant blessing in the context of the nations. This is not ethnocentrism but covenant distinctiveness: the people chosen to bear the divine name will be visibly blessed as a sign of YHWH's faithfulness — which is itself the means of the nations eventually coming to know him.
7.15: מַחֲלֶה מִצְרַיִם (maḥăleh miṣrāyim) — "the diseases of Egypt." The covenant guarantee includes freedom from the diseases that afflicted Egypt — likely including the plagues but also the endemic diseases of the Nile region. YHWH's healing protection is the positive counterpart to the military protection of holy war.
7.16: וְלֹא תַחוֹס עֵינְךָ עֲלֵיהֶם (wĕlōʾ tāḥôs ʿênĕkā ʿălêhem) — "do not look on them with pity." Ḥûs (H2347) means to have regard for, to spare emotionally. The prohibition against emotional pity toward the nations targeted for ḥērem is not cruelty but the recognition that sentiment-driven compromise would undermine the very purpose of the conquest: not expelling the false worship from the land. "And do not serve their gods — for that would be a snare to you": the warning is ultimately self-protective, not triumphalist.
7.17: The rhetorical question "these nations are more powerful than I" is addressed before the emotion arises. Deuteronomy consistently anticipates the fear and doubt that will arise when Israel faces the militarily superior nations of Canaan and provides the corrective before the crisis comes.
7.18: זָכֹר תִּזְכֹּר (zākōr tizzĕkōr) — infinitive absolute + finite verb: "remember carefully/diligently." Memory of what YHWH did to Pharaoh and Egypt is the antidote to fear of the Canaanite nations. The Exodus is not ancient history but the ongoing credential of the God who fights for Israel.
7.19: הַמַּסֹּת הַגְּדֹלֹת אֲשֶׁר רָאוּ עֵינֶיךָ (hammassōt haggĕdōlōt ʾăsher rāʾû ʿênêkā) — "the great ordeals your eyes saw." The appeal is to eyewitness testimony: the signs, wonders, mighty hand, and outstretched arm are not mythology but narrated history to which the living generation can testify. This empirical grounding of faith is characteristic of Deuteronomy's rhetorical strategy.
7.20: הַצִּרְעָה (haṣṣirʿāh) — "the hornet." The divine hornet sent among the survivors is a mysterious reference — possibly the Egyptian military campaigns that weakened the Canaanites before Israel's arrival, or a literal insect agent (as in Exod 23:28; Josh 24:12), or a metaphor for divine panic-inducing judgment. Whatever the referent, it represents YHWH's own initiative supplementing Israel's military effort: the conquest is jointly divine-human.
7.21: אֵל גָּדוֹל וְנוֹרָא (ʾēl gādôl wĕnôrāʾ) — "a great and awesome God." Nôrāʾ (H3372, the Niphal participle of yārēʾ, to fear) means fear-inducing, awesome in the sense of evoking reverent dread. The phrase recurs in Neh 1:5; 9:32 and Dan 9:4 — a liturgical formula for the God who is terrifying in power and faithful in covenant.
7.22: The "little by little" (mĕʿaṭ mĕʿaṭ) conquest strategy reflects ecological wisdom: complete and immediate depopulation would leave the land to wild animals. The divine timing is not weakness but prudent management of a complex transition. This pragmatic note within the theology of holy war is characteristic of Deuteronomy's realistic ethical vision.
7.23: מְהוּמָה גְדֹלָה (mĕhûmāh gĕdōlāh) — "great panic/confusion." Mĕhûmāh (H4103) is the divinely-sent panic that immobilizes the enemy, making military defeat possible. The same term appears in the description of divine warfare throughout the OT (1 Sam 14:15; Zech 14:13). It is YHWH's weapon before Israel strikes: the conquest is YHWH's victory first, Israel's second.
7.24: מָחָה אֶת שְׁמָם (māḥāh ʾet šĕmām) — "erase their name." The erasure of a name from "under heaven" is the reversal of covenant existence: to have a name is to have continuing presence and legacy; to have one's name erased is complete covenant annihilation. This is the ultimate ḥērem sanction.
7.25: פְּסִילֵי אֱלֹהֵיהֶם תִּשְׂרְפוּן בָּאֵשׁ (pĕsîlê ʾĕlōhêhem tisrĕpûn bāʾēsh) — "burn the carved images of their gods with fire." The fire-destruction of idols is the inverse of YHWH's theophanic fire: the divine fire that spoke from Horeb now destroys the images that would rival YHWH. Tôʿēbāh lYhwh ("a detestable thing to the LORD") — the same term (tôʿēbāh, H8441, abomination) used for Israel's dietary prohibitions and sexual violations: idols belong to the same category of covenant-contaminating objects.
7.26: חֵרֶם תִּהְיֶה כָּמֹהוּ (ḥērem tihyeh kāmōhû) — "you will be devoted to destruction along with it." The principle of contamination-by-association: bringing a ḥērem-object into the household transfers the ḥērem status to the household. This is not magic but covenantal reality: the object that belongs to YHWH for destruction cannot be assimilated to the covenant community without destroying that community. The story of Achan (Josh 7) is the narrative demonstration of this principle.

14.2: ki am qadosh atah l'YHWH Eloheicha uvcha bachar YHWH lihyot lo l'am segulah mikol ha'amim asher al-pnei ha'adamah (kî ʿam qādôš ʾattāh laYhwh ʾĕlōhêkā ûbĕkā bāḥar Yhwh lihyôt lô lĕʿam sĕgullāh mikkōl hāʿammîm ʾăšer ʿal-pĕnê hāʾădāmāh): 'For you are a people holy to the LORD your God, and the LORD has chosen you to be a people for his treasured possession, out of all the peoples who are on the face of the earth.' Am qadosh (holy people) and am segulah (treasured possession/people of special property) are Deuteronomy's twin covenant-identity terms. Segulah (also Exod 19:5; Deut 7:6; 26:18; Mal 3:17) derives from a root meaning private or personal property that one keeps close — it describes the intimate ownership-affection that distinguishes this people from all others. Peter applies both terms directly to the church (1 Pet 2:9-10): a holy nation, a people for YHWH's own possession. The dietary laws that follow in 14:3-21 are grounded in this identity: what Israel eats is shaped by who they are — a people set apart for YHWH.
14.21: lo-tevashel gedi bachalev imo (lōʾ tĕbaššēl gĕdî baḥălēb ʾimmô): 'You shall not boil a young goat in its mother's milk.' This brief prohibition (repeated verbatim in Exod 23:19; 34:26) is the ancient scriptural basis for the rabbinic separation of meat and dairy foods (basar bechalav). Its precise rationale is debated: (1) a Canaanite fertility-ritual prohibition (though no such Ugaritic ritual is clearly attested); (2) a humanitarian principle against mixing life-food (milk) with death-food (meat) especially from the same animal; (3) a holiness-distinction principle consistent with Deuteronomy's separation-categories. The threefold repetition across Torah gives it canonical weight beyond its brevity. The NT never addresses it directly, but Paul's principle in 1 Cor 8-10 (idol food) applies similar logic: covenant identity shapes eating practices, and the offense to conscience may matter more than the intrinsic act.

15.1: mikets sheva-shanim ta'aseh shemitah (miqqēṣ šebaʿ-šānîm taʿăśeh šĕmiṭṭāh): 'At the end of every seven years you shall grant a release.' Shemittah from the root šāmaṭ (to let drop, to release, to let fall) — used elsewhere for letting a sword fall (2 Sam 6:6 variant) or releasing an animal. The sabbatical year (shnat hashemittah) is the seventh year in which debts between Israelites are released and the land is allowed to rest (Exod 23:10-11; Lev 25:1-7). The year of release images the Jubilee (Lev 25:10: deror, release/liberty) — which Jesus applies to his own ministry in Luke 4:18-19 (shevat shevuyim deror, proclaim liberty to captives). The whole sabbatical-Jubilee complex encodes the principle of periodic economic reset as a covenantal obligation.
15.11: ki lo-yechdal evyon mikerev ha'aretz al-ken anochi metzavecha lemor pato'ach tiftach et-yadcha le'achicha la'aniyecha ule'evyonecha be'artzecha (kî lōʾ-yeḥdal ʾebyôn miqqereb hāʾāreṣ ʿal-kēn ʾānōkî mĕṣawwĕkā lēmōr pātōaḥ tip̄taḥ ʾet-yādĕkā lĕʾāḥîkā lĕʿăniyyĕkā ûlĕʾebyônĕkā bĕʾarṣĕkā): 'For there will never cease to be poor in the land. Therefore I command you, You shall open wide your hand to your brother, to the needy and to the poor, in your land.' Jesus cites this verse twice: Matt 26:11 and Mark 14:7 (the poor you always have with you, but you will not always have me) — not as a fatalistic dismissal of poverty but as a citation of Deut 15:11 to make a Christological point about the unique and irreplaceable moment of his physical presence. The double infinitive absolute pātōaḥ tip̄taḥ (open wide, you shall open) is intensifying idiom — categorical generosity, not selective charity.

16.1: shamor et-chodesh ha'aviv ve'asita Pesach l'YHWH Eloheicha ki be'chodesh ha'aviv hotziacha YHWH Eloheicha miMitzrayim laylah (šāmôr ʾet-ḥōdeš hāʾābîb wĕʿāśîtā peseḥ laYhwh ʾĕlōhêkā kî bĕḥōdeš hāʾābîb hôṣîʾăkā Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā mimmmiṣrayim lāylāh): 'Observe the month of Abib and keep the Passover to the LORD your God, for in the month of Abib the LORD your God brought you out of Egypt by night.' Chodesh ha'aviv (month of the green ears/spring grain) — later called Nisan in the post-exilic calendar. Pesach (Passover) from pasach (to pass over, to skip; also possibly to protect or leap over). The Passover is the founding festival of Israel's identity as a redeemed people: the night of exodus (laylah) is the pivot of the nation's existence. Paul explicitly identifies Christ as the Passover lamb (1 Cor 5:7: our Passover lamb, Christ, has been sacrificed), and John times the crucifixion to coincide with the slaughter of the Passover lambs (John 19:14,31).
16.20: tzedek tzedek tirdof lema'an tichyeh veyarashta et-ha'aretz asher YHWH Eloheicha noten lach (ṣedeq ṣedeq tirdōp̄ lĕmaʿan tiḥyeh wĕyāraštā ʾet-hāʾāreṣ ʾăšer Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā nōtēn lāk): 'Justice, and only justice, you shall pursue, that you may live and inherit the land that the LORD your God is giving you.' Tzedek tzedek tirdof — the doubled noun is for emphasis (justice, justice!) rather than mere redundancy: the repetition insists on justice that is thoroughgoing, not merely formal. Tirdof (you shall pursue/chase) is hunting vocabulary — active, vigorous pursuit of justice as one pursues prey, not passive compliance when convenient. The connection between justice and land-possession (lema'an tikhyeh ve-yarashta) makes the covenant's social ethics directly tied to covenant standing in the land. Amos 5:24 and Micah 6:8 echo the same conviction that covenant fidelity is measured in justice done to the vulnerable.

17.6: al-pi shnayim edim o shloshah edim yumot hamet al-pi ed echad lo yumot (ʿal-pî šĕnayim ʿēdîm ʾô šĕlōšāh ʿēdîm yûmat hammēt ʿal-pî ʿēd ʾeḥād lōʾ yûmat): 'On the evidence of two witnesses or of three witnesses the one who is to die shall be put to death; a person shall not be put to death on the testimony of one witness.' The multiple-witness principle appears throughout Torah (Num 35:30; Deut 19:15) and is the evidentiary foundation of Israelite jurisprudence. Jesus applies it to church discipline (Matt 18:16: if he does not listen, take one or two others along, so that every charge may be established by the evidence of two or three witnesses). Paul cites the exact formula in 2 Cor 13:1 and 1 Tim 5:19, extending it to apostolic authority and elder accountability. John 8:17 has Jesus cite it to validate his own self-testimony: in your Law it is written that the testimony of two people is true; I am the one who bears witness about myself, and the Father bears witness about me — claiming divine corroboration that meets the Torah standard.
17.18: vehayah be'shivto al-kise mamlakhto vekatav lo et-mishneh hatorah hazot al-sefer milifnei hakohanim halevi'im (wĕhāyāh kĕšibtô ʿal kissēʾ mamlaktô wĕkātab lô ʾet-mišnēh hattôrāh hazzōʾt ʿal-sēp̄er miliprĕnê hakkōhănîm hallwîyîm): 'And when he sits on the throne of his kingdom, he shall write for himself in a book a copy of this law, approved by the Levitical priests.' Mishneh hatorah (copy/repetition of this law) — this phrase is often thought to be the Hebrew original behind the Greek deuteronomion, giving the book its Greek name Deuteronomy. The king must personally copy the Torah (katav lo, write for himself) and read it all the days of his life (v. 19) so that his heart is not lifted up above his brothers. No hereditary autocracy; the king is bound by the same covenant-word as every Israelite. The ideal king is therefore not the triumphant warrior but the Torah-student who rules under the law. Psalm 119 may be read as the meditation of such a king; and the NT presents Jesus as the one who fulfills the law (Matt 5:17) from inside it.

8.1: kol-hamitzvah (kol-hammitswāh): 'every commandment' — the singular collective noun treats the entire law as one unified demand, not a menu of options. The opening command is tishmeru la'asot ('be careful to do'), the twin verbs of covenant obedience: guarding (shamar) and performing (asah). The purpose clause — 'so that you may live and multiply and take possession' — makes obedience the path to the promised inheritance; the three verbs describe ascending covenantal fullness.
8.2: anah (ʿānāh): 'to humble, afflict' — the wilderness is here interpreted as intentional divine pedagogy; YHWH afflicted Israel lema'an da'at et-asher bi-levavcha, 'to know what was in your heart.' Nasah (nāsāh): 'to test' — not to acquire information YHWH lacked, but to reveal to Israel itself what its heart contained. The wilderness is the diagnostic chamber of the covenant, not a punishment for Sinai apostasy alone.
8.3: lo al-halachem levaddo yichyeh ha-adam ki al-kol-motsa pi-YHWH: 'Not by bread alone does man live, but by every word from the mouth of YHWH.' The manna (man) is a word-substitute — it teaches that divine provision flows through divine speech, not natural process. Jesus quotes this verse verbatim (Deut LXX) at his first temptation (Matt 4:4), redirecting Satan's bread-test to the prior authority of the word that already commands and provides. The motsa ('going forth, utterance') of YHWH's mouth is the creative and sustaining word behind all physical provision.
8.4: simlathecha lo valah (simlāṯĕkā lōʾ bāletāh): 'your garment did not wear out' — valah means to decay, wear out, become old (the same root in 'vile'); the preservation of clothing alongside manna provision extends the miracle into every dimension of physical existence. The forty-year span frames the miracle: nothing natural survives forty years of wilderness use. The verse stands without elaboration — its brevity is its witness.
8.5: yasar (yāsar): 'to discipline, instruct, chasten' — the father-son metaphor for the wilderness discipline is the theological key to chapter 8. Ka'asher yeyaser ish et-beno, 'as a man disciplines his son' — paternal discipline is the interpretive framework for Exodus hardship. Proverbs 3:11-12 develops this directly (musar YHWH beni al-tima'as, 'do not despise the LORD's discipline'); Hebrews 12:5-11 quotes Prov 3:11-12 and applies it to Christian suffering, completing the typological arc that begins here.
8.6: lalechet bidrachav (lāleket bidrākāyw): 'to walk in his ways' — the walk-metaphor (halakh) is Deuteronomy's primary ethics image; the covenant life is conceived as a path to travel, not a set of rules to memorize. Three covenant acts are specified: keeping (shamar), walking (halakh), fearing (yare') — cognitive, behavioral, and affective dimensions of the covenant relationship.
8.7: eretz tovah (ʾereṣ ṭôbāh): 'a good land' — the threefold repetition of tov in Deut 8:7-10 (good land, v7; you eat and are satisfied, v10; the good land, v10) echoes Genesis 1's creation declaration. The land is described through its water systems: nachalei mayim (streams of water), ayanot (springs), tehomot (deeps/underground sources) — a complete hydrological portrait. The eschatological land of promise is paradigmatically a well-watered garden.
8.8: The seven species — wheat, barley, vines, figs, pomegranates, olive oil, and honey — constitute Israel's agricultural canon. These are not random but are the covenantally significant products of the land; the later agricultural festivals (firstfruits, Pentecost, Tabernacles) are organized around them. Devash (dĕbaš): 'honey' — may be date-honey rather than bee-honey; in any case, the sweetness principle: the land's produce is not merely adequate but pleasurable.
8.9: lo techsar kol-ba (lōʾ teḥsar kol-bāh): 'you will lack nothing in it' — the categorical sufficiency of the land; nothing is missing. The geological detail — rocks containing iron (barzel), hills yielding copper (nechoshet) — specifies material abundance in a pre-industrial economy where metal was the index of civilizational power. The Promised Land is both agriculturally and industrially complete.
8.10: berakhta et-YHWH Eloheicha (ûbēraktā ʾeṯ-Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā): 'you shall bless the LORD your God' — the command to bless YHWH after eating (ve-akhalta ve-savata) is the rabbinic basis for the Birkat Hamazon (grace after meals), considered a Toraitic obligation. The sequence — eat, be satisfied, bless — reverses the anxiety sequence of hoarding: you may eat fully because the giver is reliable, and the proper response to satiation is gratitude rather than expanded appetite.
8.11: pen tishkach et-YHWH Eloheicha (pen-tiškaḥ ʾeṯ-Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā): 'beware lest you forget the LORD your God' — the forget/remember axis is the backbone of Deuteronomy. Zakhor (remember) and shakhach (forget) are the two poles of covenant faithfulness and failure. Forgetting YHWH is defined specifically as failing to keep the commandments (lishmore mitzvotav u-mishpatav ve-chuqqotav) — it is not primarily an intellectual lapse but a behavioral drift.
8.12: The prosperity spiral begins: eating to the full, building fine houses, settling. Batim tovim (bāttîm ṭôbîm): 'fine houses' — the same tov (good/fine) that characterized the land now describes human constructions; the danger is when man's productions rival the land's gift. The five verbs (ate, built, settled, increased, multiplied, v13) trace the arc of prosperity that threatens covenant memory.
8.13: Material expansion continues — the triad of flock, silver, gold — the classic markers of patriarchal prosperity (cf. Gen 13:2, Abraham). Kesef ve-zahav (kesep wĕzāhāb): 'silver and gold' — the two monetary metals that summarize wealth. The expansion is not condemned; prosperity is a covenant blessing. The danger lies not in the wealth but in the heart-response it generates (v14).
8.14: veram levavecha (wĕrām lĕbābĕkā): 'your heart will become proud/lifted up' — rum is elevation; pride is the heart lifting itself above its proper creaturely position. The result is forgetting YHWH (vishkachta et-YHWH Eloheicha). The anatomy of apostasy: prosperity → proud heart → forgetting → idolatry/disobedience. The identification of Egypt as beit avadim (house of slaves) reminds that the prosperity was not earned but given from a state of utter dependency.
8.15: nachash saraph (nāḥāš śārāp): 'venomous/fiery serpent' — saraph means burning, fiery; probably describing the burning sensation of the venom (cf. Num 21:6-9, the bronze serpent). The wilderness is not merely difficult but actively hostile — snakes, scorpions, thirst, no water. Against this backdrop, the flint-water miracle (hamotz'i lecha mayim mi-tsur hachalamish) is the measure of YHWH's transforming provision: water from the hardest rock.
8.16: The manna returns as interpretive summary: its purpose was le'anothecha u-lensasothecha ('to humble and test you'), leheitiv lecha be'acharitecha ('to do you good in the end'). The wilderness suffering is teleological — oriented toward a good end that the sufferer cannot see in the moment. This is the theodicy of Deuteronomy 8: affliction serves formation, formation serves inheritance.
8.17: kochi ve'otsem yadi asah li et-hachayil hazeh (kōḥî wĕʿōṣem yādî ʿāśāh lî ʾeṯ-haḥayil hazzeh): 'My power and the strength of my hands produced this wealth for me' — the formula of self-sufficiency; the precise inversion of covenant acknowledgment. Chayil means both wealth and valor/strength; the self-made-man ideology claims that human chayil generates its own chayil. This verse is the theological ground of every false gospel of self-reliance.
8.18: koach la'asot chayil (kōaḥ laʿăśôṯ ḥāyil): 'ability to produce wealth' — the corrective: productive capacity itself is YHWH's gift. The purpose is specified: lema'an hakim et-brito, 'in order to confirm his covenant' — the covenant, not human ambition, is the framework within which prosperity occurs. What looks like human achievement is YHWH keeping his Abrahamic promise; wealth as covenant faithfulness demonstration.
8.19: avod tovedun (ʾābōd tōbēdûn): 'you will certainly perish' — the absolute infinitive + finite verb construction is Hebrew's device for emphasis and certainty. The apostasy chain: forget YHWH → follow other gods → serve them → bow down to them — four steps of progressive idolatrous commitment. Each step deepens the covenant breach; the terminus is national death. The ha'idoti vakhem hayom ('I solemnly warn you today') invokes the treaty-witness formula.
8.20: The nations-parallel closes the chapter: Israel will perish ka-goyim (like the nations) — not as a special covenant people but as just another failed kingdom — asher YHWH mavid mipneihem (which YHWH is destroying before you). The nations are destroyed for their covenant violations with the created order; Israel can be destroyed for its covenant violation with YHWH. Covenant privilege does not exempt from covenant accountability.

9.1: shema Yisrael (šĕmaʿ yiśrāʾēl): 'Hear, O Israel' — the Shema-formula opens every major address in Deuteronomy; hearing (obedient attention) is the covenant stance. La'avotar goyim (lĕhaḵrît gôyim): 'to dispossess nations greater and mightier' — gadol (greater) and atzum (mightier) are exactly the terms used at Kadesh-barnea to describe the paralyzing impossibility; now they introduce a possibility in YHWH's strength.
9.2: The Anakites (anakim) — the giant-descendants of Anak; the spies' report had made them the paradigm of military impossibility (Num 13:33: 'we were like grasshoppers in our own eyes'). The proverb 'mi yityatzev lifnei benei Anak?' ('who can stand before the sons of Anak?') preserves the cultural terror these warriors generated. Deuteronomy answers the rhetorical question: YHWH can, and Israel will, because YHWH fights for them.
9.3: esh okhlah (ʾēš ʾōkelāh): 'a consuming fire' — the first occurrence of this theophanic description in Deuteronomy 9; it recurs in 4:24 and Heb 12:29. The fire-character of YHWH is his holiness in active engagement with what resists him. Ha-over lefanecha ('going before you') — YHWH as the advance combat force; Israel follows the divine warrior's trail. The promise is triple: destroy (yashmid), subdue (yakniyam), and drive out (vehorashtam).
9.4: al-tomar bilevavcha betzidqati (ʾal-tōmar bilibabĕkā bĕṣidqāṯî): 'do not say in your heart because of my righteousness' — the conquest of Canaan tempts Israel to interpret divine favor as moral achievement. The correction: the nations' dispossession is berishtat haggoyim ('because of the wickedness of these nations'), not Israel's merit. This verse is the anthropological ground for Paul's argument in Romans: before God, no one can say 'because of my righteousness.'
9.5: lo betzidqatcha uveyosher levavcha (lōʾ bĕṣidqāṯĕkā ûbĕyōšer lĕbābĕkā): 'not because of your righteousness or the uprightness of your heart' — the double denial eliminates both behavioral righteousness (tzaddiq) and motivational integrity (yashar) as grounds for the gift. The positive ground is twofold: (1) berishtat haggoyim (the nations' wickedness); (2) lema'an hakim et-hadavar (to fulfill the covenant oath to the patriarchs). Grace, not merit, is always the covenantal operative.
9.6: am qesheh oref (ʿam qĕšēh-ʿōrep): 'stiff-necked people' — the defining characterization that Moses levels at Israel; the ox that will not yield its neck to the yoke. This designation first appears at Exod 32:9 after the golden calf; Deuteronomy 9 returns to it as the theological counter to any self-congratulatory interpretation of the conquest. Stephen's use of this exact phrase in Acts 7:51 shows its enduring force as the diagnosis of Israel's pattern of resistance.
9.7: zakhor al-tishkach (zĕkor ʾal-tiškaḥ): 'remember, do not forget' — the two imperatives set up the antithetical pairing that structures Deuteronomic ethics. Mamrim heyitem ('you have been rebellious'): marah means to rebel, to be bitter, to be defiant — the full weight of covenant-legal disobedience. Meyom da'ati etchem ('from the day I knew you') — Moses's pastoral knowledge as witness; the entire history is a cumulative record.
9.8: Chorev (ḥōrēb): Deuteronomy's name for Sinai; the same site where YHWH revealed himself in fire and gave the law. Le'hashmidechem ('to destroy you'): the same root (shamad) used for the nations Israel will displace. The parallel is deliberate: Israel's faithlessness makes them liable to the same destruction as the nations. The difference is intercessory grace (v18-19, 25-29).
9.9: luchot avanim luchot ha-berit (luḥôṯ ʾăbānîm luḥôṯ habbĕrîṯ): 'tablets of stone, the covenant tablets' — the apposition identifies the stone tablets as the material form of the covenant; to receive the tablets is to receive the covenant. Lechem lo akhalty umayim lo shatiti ('bread I did not eat and water I did not drink') — the complete fast matches the duration; Moses sustained on divine presence alone, a proleptic sign of the eschatological feast where YHWH is the provision.
9.10: ketuvim be-etzbah Elohim (kĕṯûbîm bĕʾeṣbaʿ ʾĕlōhîm): 'written by the finger of God' — the identical phrase as Exod 31:18; the Decalogue's divine authorship is emphasized precisely where it would become most important: Israel has just demonstrated why they need a law they cannot write themselves. The etzbah (finger) of God that writes the law is the same etzbah that drove out Egypt's plagues (Exod 8:19) and that Jesus invokes in Luke 11:20.
9.11: The forty days concluded: vayyitten YHWH elai et-shney luchot ha-avanim luchot ha-berit ('the LORD gave me the two stone tablets, the covenant tablets'). The handing over (natan) is formal and deliberate — the covenant document transferred from divine hand to human mediator. Verse 11 is the structural hinge: the tablets given just as the people below have abandoned them.
9.12: maher saru min-haderekh (māher sārû min-haddārek): 'they have quickly turned from the way I commanded them' — maher (quickly, soon) is the word of shocking speed: Moses has not even descended with the covenant tablets when Israel constructs its replacement. Pesel masekah ('cast metal idol') — the melted earrings shaped into the calf constitute the precise inversion of the covenant tablets: human-made versus God-written, metal image versus invisible word.
9.13: am qesheh oref (ʿam qĕšēh-ʿōrep): 'a stiff-necked people' — repeated from v6 for emphasis; YHWH's own diagnosis of Israel's character. The repeated assessment both explains the apostasy (they are constitutionally resistant) and grounds the need for grace (there is no remedy from within Israel's own nature). This is the OT anthropology that Paul assumes in Romans 7.
9.14: herepha mimmenni veashmidem (herepeʾ mimmennî wĕʾašmîdēm): 'leave me alone that I may destroy them' — YHWH presents himself as restrained by Moses's presence; the intercessory posture of the mediator as the space between divine wrath and its object. Emcheh et-shemam ('I will blot out their name'): name-erasure is covenant death — to have no name in Israel is to have no future; compare Rev 3:5 (not blot out from the book of life). The Moses-replacement offer (a mightier nation from Moses) anticipates the new creation community.
9.15: Moses descends veha-har bor be-esh ('while the mountain was burning with fire'), the two covenant tablets in his hands — the physical tension between the covenant-document in his grip and the covenant-broken scene below. The fire-mountain is the covenant's witness; Moses descends from YHWH's presence into the catastrophe that presence indicts.
9.16: chet'em laYHWH Eloheichem (ḥăṭāʾtem laYhwh ʾĕlōhêkem): 'you had sinned against the LORD your God' — the covenant-legal vocabulary; chata is missing the mark, failing the covenant standard. Egel masekah ('a cast calf') — the bull-image of El/YHWH syncretism; not the worship of another god but the worship of YHWH under a prohibited form. The sin is representational before it is substitutional.
9.17: Moses's tablet-breaking: va'etpos bishnei haluchot va'ashlikh otam me'al shetei yadai ('I seized the two tablets and threw them from both my hands') — the enacted covenant-dissolution; shattering the treaty document was the ancient Near Eastern symbolic act for covenant termination. Moses breaks the tablets before the people's eyes as a prophetic sign-act: you have already broken what these tablets contain. The breaking is not anger management but theology in action.
9.18: The second prostration — forty days and forty nights; the threefold parallel with v9 and v25 structures the intercession narrative. Kol-chatatechem asher chatatum la'asot hara be'einei YHWH ('all your sin that you committed by doing what was evil in the sight of the LORD') — the comprehensive summary; ra be'einei YHWH ('evil in the eyes of YHWH') is Deuteronomy's standard formula for covenant breach.
9.19: chamat YHWH ve-apo (ḥămaṯ Yhwh wĕʾappô): 'the fury of the LORD and his anger' — the pairing of chemah (burning wrath) and af (anger/nostril-flaring) intensifies the danger; YHWH's wrath is not mild discomfort but covenant-breach consequence. Vayyishma YHWH elai gam bappa'am hazeh ('the LORD listened to me that time as well') — the effectiveness of intercession; prayer changes the divine-human trajectory. Moses's intercessory effectiveness is the OT type of Christ's high-priestly prayer (Heb 7:25).
9.20: be'Aharon hitanaf meod l'hashmido (bĕʾahărōn hiṯʾannaṯ mĕʾōd lĕhašmîdô): 'very angry with Aaron to destroy him' — Aaron's priestly leadership of the calf-construction makes him secondarily liable for the covenant disaster. Moses intercedes specifically for Aaron (va'etpallel gam be'ad Aharon ba'et hahiv, 'I prayed also for Aaron at that same time'). The high priest is saved by intercessory prayer; the greater High Priest (Heb 5:1) is himself the intercessor who cannot be destroyed.
9.21: The destruction of the calf follows a precise protocol: burned (saraph ba'esh), crushed (khatash), ground fine (tachon heitev), scattered in the stream. The thoroughness mirrors the herem-destruction of Canaanite idols commanded in Deut 7:5, 25. Total destruction is the only adequate response to the idol; partial preservation leaves the possibility of return. The dust thrown into the stream recalls Exod 32:20 — the consuming of the idol by its former worshippers.
9.22: The catalog of wilderness failures: Taberah (tab'erah, 'burning' — the fire incident of Num 11:1-3), Massah (massah, 'testing' — the water-from-rock complaint of Exod 17:1-7), Kibroth-hattaavah (qibrot hatta'avah, 'graves of craving' — the quail-excess of Num 11:31-35). Each place-name is a theological memory: the wilderness geography is sin-geography, the landscape of Israel's pattern of provoking YHWH.
9.23: lo-he'emantem lo velo shmahtem beqolo (lōʾ heʾĕmantem lô wĕlōʾ šmaʿtem bĕqōlô): 'you did not trust him and did not obey his voice' — the two verbs of covenant failure at Kadesh: unbelief (aman hiphil, to trust/believe) and disobedience (shama to listen/obey). The Kadesh failure is not primarily military but theological: the spies' report was believed over YHWH's promise. Hebrews 3:16-19 uses this as the definitive example of the unbelief-disobedience connection.
9.24: mamrim heyitem im-YHWH meyom da'ati etchem (mamrim hĕyîtem ʿim-Yhwh miyyôm daʿtî ʾeṯkem): 'you have been rebellious against the LORD from the day I first knew you' — the sweeping pastoral indictment; Moses's entire relationship with Israel has been a relationship with rebels. Da'ati ('from my knowing you') frames it relationally: Moses knows Israel not as a supervisor knows employees but as a shepherd knows sheep. The rebellion is all the more grievous for its relational context.
9.25: The second extended intercession: forty days and forty nights, prostrate before YHWH (va'etnapal lifnei YHWH et arba'im hayom ve'et arba'im halaylah asher hitnapalti). The three prostration periods (vv9, 18, 25) structure chapter 9's narrative of grace through intercession. Moses's physical posture — face-to-the-ground — enacts the absolute vulnerability of the mediator before the covenant-keeping God on behalf of the covenant-breaking people.
9.26: Adonai YHWH al-tashchet ammecha venachalatecha (ʾăḏōnāy Yhwh ʾal-tašḥēṯ ʿammĕkā wĕnaḥălāṯekā): 'O Lord GOD, do not destroy your people and your inheritance' — the double divine name (Adonai YHWH) of solemn address; the same formula Moses used at Deut 3:24. The intercessory argument is possessive: they are your people, your inheritance. YHWH's own honor is at stake in their preservation. Asher padita begadolecha ('whom you redeemed by your greatness'): the exodus-redemption as the basis for the appeal.
9.27: zechor la'avadeicha le-Avraham le-Yitzchak ule-Ya'aqov (zĕkor lĕʿăbādêkā lĕʾabrāhām lĕyiṣḥāq ûlĕyaʿăqōb): 'remember your servants Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob' — the covenant-history argument; the patriarchal promise creates a divine obligation that cannot be abandoned without self-contradiction. Al-tefen el-qeshiyut ha'am hazeh ('do not consider the stubbornness of this people') — Moses asks YHWH to look past the present character evidence to the prior covenant commitment.
9.28: pen yomru ha'aretz asher hotzeitanu mishsam (pen-yōmrû hāʾāreṣ ʾăšer hôṣēʾṯānû miššām): 'lest the land from which you brought us say...' — the nations-as-audience argument; the watching world will interpret Israel's destruction as evidence of YHWH's inability (mibli yecholet YHWH, 'because YHWH was not able') or hatred (umissin'ato otam). Divine reputation in the watching world is grounds for covenant preservation. This argument recurs in Moses's intercession at Num 14:13-16 and in the exile-passages of Ezek 36:20-23.
9.29: vehem ammecha venachalatecha (wĕhēm ʿammĕkā wĕnaḥălāṯekā): 'but they are your people and your inheritance' — the closing possessive assertion; the intercessory prayer ends with the same possessive argument it began with (v26). The prayer's logic: they belong to you, you redeemed them, your reputation requires their survival, your covenant to the patriarchs demands their continuation. The bare possessives — your people, your inheritance — are the ultimate appeal: ownership creates obligation.

10.1: pesol lecha shney luchot avanim karishonim (psol lĕkā šĕnê-luḥôṯ ʾăbānîm kāriʾšōnîm): 'chisel out two stone tablets like the first ones' — the covenant renewal requires human preparation; Moses chisels, YHWH writes. The distinction preserves both human agency and divine authorship in the covenant-making process. Aron etz ('a wooden chest') — the first mention of the ark's construction in Deuteronomy; its purpose is to house the covenant document, making the covenant portable and central to Israel's movement.
10.2: ektov al-haluchot et-hadevarim asher hayu al-haluchot harishonim (wĕʾeḵtōb ʿal-halluḥôṯ ʾeṯ-haddĕbārîm ʾăšer hāyû ʿal-halluḥôṯ hāriʾšōnîm): 'I will write on the tablets the same words as were on the first ones' — the covenant is renewed without revision; grace restores without lowering the standard. The broken tablets represented covenant rupture; the new tablets represent covenant renewal on identical terms — not a new, easier arrangement but the same demanding covenant given again.
10.3: va'a'as aron atzei shittim (wāʾaʿaś ʾărôn ʿăṣê šiṭṭîm): 'I made an ark of acacia wood' — shittim (acacia) is the same wood specified in Exod 25:10 for the tabernacle ark; Moses makes a simpler version here. The narrative conflates or summarizes the tabernacle construction; the key point is that the ark precedes the tablets — covenant-storage is prepared before covenant-document is received. Preparation for receiving the word of God is itself a covenant act.
10.4: aseret ha-devarim (ʿăśereṯ haddĕbārîm): 'the ten words/commands' — the technical OT term for the Decalogue (used also at Exod 34:28, Deut 4:13); devarim (words/matters) rather than mitzvot (commandments) emphasizes their character as divine speech before they are legal demands. Written again by YHWH from the fire; the second giving of the Decalogue is an enacted grace — the destroyed covenant document replaced by the covenant-keeping God.
10.5: va'asem et-haluchot ba'aron (wāʾāśem ʾeṯ-halluḥôṯ bāʾārôn): 'I placed the tablets in the ark' — the ark as the covenant's home; the law at the center of the community's physical structure. The closing note — vayyihyu sham ka'asher tzivvani YHWH ('and there they remain, just as the LORD commanded me') — grounds continuing covenant faithfulness on the original divine command, not periodic human recommitment.
10.6: The travel notation records Aaron's death at Moserah (cf. Num 33:30-38 where Hor is the site; the discrepancy may reflect different naming traditions for the same or adjacent sites). Va'yekahein Eleazar beno tachtav ('Eleazar his son took over the priestly ministry'): the Aaronic priesthood continues through biological succession; the covenant institution survives the death of its founding figure. Death does not interrupt the divine program — it transfers to the next generation.
10.7: The travel list — Gudgodah (gudgodah) to Jotbathah (yotvathah), a land of flowing streams (eretz nachalei mayim) — provides geographical specificity that resists abstraction. The mention of water-rich Jotbathah after the wilderness narrative is a micro-movement from scarcity to provision; the promise-land motif appears in the travelogue itself.
10.8: hivdil YHWH et-shevet ha-Levi (hibdîl Yhwh ʾeṯ-šēbeṯ halēwî): 'the LORD set apart the tribe of Levi' — hivdil (to separate, set apart) is the vocabulary of holiness-creation; the Levites are made holy by divine designation, not natural superiority. Three functions enumerated: (1) carry the ark (laset et-aron berit YHWH); (2) stand before YHWH to minister (la'amed lifnei YHWH lesharto); (3) bless in his name (levarekh bishmov). The priestly function is simultaneously spatial (standing before), relational (serving), and verbal (blessing).
10.9: YHWH hu nachalato (Yhwh hûʾ naḥălāṯô): 'the LORD himself is his inheritance' — the Levitical principle: no land portion, YHWH instead. This is the highest possible exchange: finite territory given up for the infinite God. The principle is eschatological: Rev 21:3 ('they will be his people and God himself will be with them') generalizes the Levitical condition to all of the new creation people. In Christ, all believers become a royal priesthood (1 Pet 2:9) for whom God is the inheritance.
10.10: velo ava YHWH hashchetecha (wĕlōʾ-ʾābāh Yhwh hašḥîṯĕkā): 'the LORD was unwilling to destroy you' — the intercession's result; YHWH's will is changed by prayer. This is one of the Bible's most direct statements that intercessory prayer alters the divine course of action. The theological question (does prayer change God?) is answered here: yes, within the framework of covenant faithfulness. Moses's successful intercession is the OT type of Christ's ever-living intercession (Heb 7:25).
10.11: qum lekh le'neso'a ha'am (qûm lēk lĕnasoaʿ hāʿām): 'get up and lead the people on their journey' — the renewal enables mission; after intercession comes commission. The covenant forgiveness does not merely restore the status quo ante but recommissions Moses and Israel to the original purpose. Asher nishbati la'avotam latet lahem ('which I swore to give to their fathers') — the inheritance-promise is renewed; the failure did not cancel the intention.
10.12: mah YHWH Eloheicha sho'el me'imcha (mā Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā šōʾēl mēʿimmāk): 'what does the LORD your God ask of you?' — the great rhetorical question; the answer is the Deuteronomic summary of the covenant life. Five verbs: fear (liyre'ah), walk (lalechet), love (le'ahavah), serve (la'avod) with all your heart and soul, and keep (lishmor) the commandments. Micah 6:8 compresses this to three: justice, loyalty, humble walking. Jesus further compresses it to two (love God, love neighbor), calling these 'all the Law and the Prophets.'
10.13: letov lach (lĕṭôb lāk): 'for your good' — the covenant commandments are not arbitrary impositions but the path to human flourishing. The covenant law is characterized throughout Deuteronomy as gift before obligation: YHWH gives the commandments for your own good. Paul's statement in Romans 7:12 (the law is holy and the commandment is holy, righteous, and good) restores this positive characterization after his analysis of the law's limitations.
10.14: YHWH Eloheicha hashamayim ushmei hashamayim ha'aretz vekhol-asher bah (Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā haššāmayim ûšmê haššāmayim hāʾāreṣ wĕkol-ʾăšer-bāh): 'To the LORD your God belong the heavens, even the highest heavens, the earth and everything in it' — the universal sovereignty statement; shmei hashamayim ('the heavens of heavens') is Hebrew superlative: the uttermost sky. The paradox of the next verse: this cosmic God specifically chose Abraham's descendants among all peoples. Universal sovereignty and particular election are not in tension; they explain each other.
10.15: raq ba'avotecha chashaq YHWH le'ahavah otam (raq bāʾăbōṯêkā ḥāšaq Yhwh lĕʾahăbāh ʾōṯām): 'yet the LORD delighted in your fathers and loved them' — chashaq is a strong word: attachment, desire, binding affection; it appears only 11 times in the OT and always carries emotional intensity. The election love is prior (raq = 'only', 'nevertheless' — despite their smallness) and generational (the patriarchs chosen, their descendants chosen in them). This is sovereign grace: the object does not explain the choice.
10.16: umaltem et arlat levavechem (ûmaltĕṯem ʾeṯ ʿŏrlaṯ lĕbabĕkem): 'circumcise your hearts' — the inner circumcision command; the physical rite that marks covenant membership (Gen 17) points to the inner transformation it cannot produce. Arlah (foreskin/uncircumcision) applied to the heart means the same obstruction that physical uncircumcision represents: exclusion from the covenant community's inner life. Jer 4:4, 9:25-26 develop this; Romans 2:29 completes it: the true circumcision is of the heart, by the Spirit. Deuteronomy 10:16 is the OT's clearest pointer to the new covenant necessity.
10.17: Elohei ha-elohim va'Adonei ha-adonim (ʾĕlōhê hāʾĕlōhîm waʾăḏōnê hāʾăḏōnîm): 'God of gods and Lord of lords' — the double superlative; the only place in the Hebrew Bible where both titles appear together. Revelation 17:14 and 19:16 apply them both to Christ: Lord of lords and King of kings. Ha-gadol ha-gibbor veha-nora ('the great, mighty, and awesome'): the Deuteronomic divine-attribute triad that becomes the liturgical formula of the Amidah prayer and Nehemiah 9:32.
10.18: oseh mishpat yatom ve'almanah (ʿōśeh mišpaṭ yāṯôm wĕʾalmānāh): 'he upholds justice for the fatherless and the widow' — the marginalized triad of Deuteronomy: the orphan, widow, and foreigner (ger). YHWH's own character is defined by this justice-work; his ethics of inclusion precede and ground Israel's. Oheiv ger ('loves the foreigner'): YHWH's love for the outsider makes the command to love the foreigner (v19) a participation in YHWH's own disposition, not merely a humanitarian policy.
10.19: va'ahavtem et-hager ki gerim heyitem be'eretz Mitzraim (waʾăhebtem ʾeṯ-hagger kî-gērîm hĕyîṯem bĕʾereṣ miṣrāyim): 'love the foreigner, for you were foreigners in Egypt' — the experiential basis for social ethics: Israel's own memory of marginalization generates the ethical obligation toward the marginalized. This is the double-movement of covenantal compassion: what YHWH did for you when you were powerless, you must enact for others who are now powerless. The Exodus is not only a theological event but an ethical resource.
10.20: u'vo tidbaq (ûbô ṯiḏbāq): 'hold fast to him' — daveq (to cling, cleave) is the same root as Genesis 2:24's ve-davaq be'ishto ('he shall hold fast to his wife') — the marital-union vocabulary applied to the YHWH-Israel relationship. Devequt (cleaving/attachment) becomes a central category in Jewish mysticism; here it is the covenant's relational core: not merely obeying commands but cleaving to the person who gives them. Deut 10:20 is the OT ground for Paul's joined to the Lord language (1 Cor 6:17).
10.21: hu tehillatecha vehu Eloheicha (hûʾ ṯĕhillāṯĕkā wĕhûʾ ʾĕlōhêkā): 'he is your praise and he is your God' — the double identification: YHWH is both the content of Israel's praise and Israel's God. The equation of God-ness and praiseworthiness is the confession that the being who deserves praise is the one who acts for you. Asher asah ittecha et-hagedolot ve'et-hanora'ot ha'eleh asher ra'u eineicha ('who has done these great and awe-inspiring things your own eyes have seen'): the eyewitness element grounds the praise in history, not abstraction.
10.22: bishiv'im nefesh yardu avoteicha Mitzraimah (bĕšibʿîm nep̄eš yārĕḏû ʾăbōṯêkā miṣrāymāh): 'your fathers went down to Egypt as seventy souls' — the historical baseline (Exod 1:5, Gen 46:27); from seventy to as numerous as the stars (kekochvei hashamayim larov) is the Abrahamic promise fulfilled (Gen 15:5, 22:17). The numerical explosion across centuries is YHWH's demonstration that his covenant promises are not rhetorical: he counted seventy and made them millions. The God who counts the stars (Ps 147:4) counts and multiplies his covenant people.

11.1: veahavta et YHWH eloheicha (wĕʾāhabtā ʾet Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā): 'Love the LORD your God.' ʾāhab (love) is the covenant-loyalty term that integrates both emotion and action — it is not merely affective feeling but the orientation of the whole self toward the covenant partner. mišmartô (his requirements/charge): derived from šāmar (to keep, guard), denoting the whole range of covenant obligations — statutes (ḥuqqôt), ordinances (mišpāṭîm), and commandments (miṣwōt). The four-fold enumeration signals the comprehensiveness of covenant obligation.
11.2: yedatem hayom (yĕdaʿtem hayyôm): 'Know today.' The address shifts to the living generation — the children have not known (yādaʿ, the experiential-relational knowing) the exodus events. mûsar YHWH (the discipline/instruction of YHWH): mûsār denotes formative correction through experience — the hard lessons of the wilderness. The knowing that grounds obligation is grounded in witnessed history, not mere tradition.
11.3: otot umaasav (ʾōtōtāyw ûmaʿaśāyw): 'His signs and deeds.' ʾôt (sign) denotes a confirmatory marker; maʿaśeh (deed/work) the accomplished act. Together they describe the pattern of the exodus: sign and wonder pair as YHWH's credentials in Deut 4:34; 7:19; 26:8. Egypt is the theater of divine demonstration.
11.4: asher hetzif et-mei yam-suph (ʾăšer hēṣîp̄ ʾet-mê yam-sûp̄): 'Who caused the waters of the Reed Sea to overflow/engulf.' yam sûp̄: traditionally rendered Red Sea, but sûp̄ means reed — the Reed Sea or Sea of Reeds. hēṣîp̄ from ṣûp̄ (to flow over, overwhelm): the specific verb used in Ps 124:4-5 of the waters that 'would have engulfed us.' The drowning of the Egyptian army is the definitive act of holy war.
11.5: mah-asah lachem bamidbar (mâ-ʿāśâ lākem bammidbar): 'What he did to you in the wilderness.' The wilderness (midbar) is the place of YHWH's sustained provision and discipline — manna, water from the rock, quail — and the site of Israel's repeated failure. Both the provision and the punishment are part of the covenantal knowing that the current generation possesses.
11.6: asher asah leDatan velaAviram: The Dathan and Abiram episode (Num 16) — the earth swallowed them. wattiphtaḥ hāʾădāmâ ʾet-pîhā (the ground opened its mouth): the reversal of the Genesis creation in which Adam was formed from the ʾădāmâ — the earth that gave life takes it back as judgment. The covenant community is defined by remembering both the beneficence and the severity of YHWH.
11.7: kol-maaseh YHWH hagadol (kol-maʿăśēh Yhwh haggādôl): 'All the great work of YHWH.' haggādôl (the great): the emphatic divine epithet. The generation addressed has with their own eyes (ʿênêkem rōʾôt) seen all these great works — their eyewitness obligation is greater than their children's. Deuteronomy's pedagogy is grounded in the irreducible weight of direct experience.
11.8: ushmartem et-kol-hamitzvah (ûšĕmartem ʾet-kol-hammiṣwâ): 'Keep all the commandment.' mīṣwâ is singular despite the plural context — 'the commandment' as a comprehensive whole, the unified Torah-demand. The motivation: 'that you may be strong and go in and possess the land.' The indicative of redemption precedes the imperative of obedience (ch 1-11's recitation of YHWH's acts grounds the obligations of ch 12-26).
11.9: lehaʾarik yamim (lĕhaʾărîk yāmîm): 'To prolong your days.' ʾārak (to be long, to extend): the length-of-days formula that runs through Deuteronomy as the covenant-blessing consequence of obedience (5:16; 6:2; 17:20; 22:7; 25:15). The land flowing with milk and honey (ʾereṣ zābat ḥālāb ûdĕbaš): the classic double phrase for Canaan's fertility; zābat (flowing, dripping) from zûb (to flow) marks the land's natural abundance.
11.10: lo chemereṣ mitsrayim hi (lōʾ kĕʾereṣ miṣrayim hîʾ): 'Not like the land of Egypt.' The contrast is between Egypt's irrigated agriculture (dependent on the Nile, controlled by human labor: you watered it 'with your foot') and Canaan's rain-dependent agriculture (dependent entirely on YHWH's provision). The agricultural difference encodes a theological difference — Canaan requires faith in YHWH's rainfall; Egypt produces the illusion of human self-sufficiency.
11.11: ereẓ harim uveqaot (ʾereṣ hārîm ûbĕqāʿôt): 'A land of mountains and valleys.' The topography of Canaan — varied highland terrain requiring rain — contrasts with Egypt's flat flood-plain. lematar (for rain): the land 'drinks water from the rain of heaven' (šōtâ mayim lĕmaṭar haššāmayim). YHWH's eyes are upon it continuously — ʿênê Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā bāh (the eyes of YHWH your God are upon it) — making the land YHWH's ongoing concern and direct provision.
11.12: mereshit hashana (mērēšît haššānâ): 'From the beginning of the year.' The temporal expression marks YHWH's oversight as year-round — not occasional or seasonal but continuous surveillance. aḥărît šānâ (end of the year): the complete calendar under divine attention. The land is unlike Egypt's Nile-dependent cycle — Israel's agricultural success depends on YHWH's covenantal faithfulness expressed through seasonal rainfall.
11.13: veHAYA im-shamoʿa tishmeʿu (wĕhāyâ ʾim-šāmōaʿ tišmĕʿû): 'If you carefully obey.' The double infinitive absolute construction (šāmōaʿ tišmĕʿû) intensifies the obedience: 'if you listen-listening' = diligent, thoroughgoing attention. This formula introduces the covenant blessing-condition that parallels the Shema (6:4-9); obedience to love-and-serve (leʾāhăbâ... ûlĕʿobdô) earns seasonal rain.
11.14: yoreh umalkosh (yōreh ûmalqôš): 'Early rain and late rain.' The agricultural calendar of the Levant depends on two rain seasons: the yōreh (early rain, October-November, softening the ground for planting) and the malqôš (late rain, March-April, filling the grain before harvest). Both are promised as covenant blessings — YHWH controls the cycles on which Israel's survival depends. Drought is the default covenant-curse (28:22-24).
11.15: venatati esev besadcha livehemtecha: 'I will give grass in your fields for your livestock.' The abundance cascades from grain to grass to livestock to human eating — each element dependent on YHWH's rainfall provision. veʾākaltā veśābaʿtā (you will eat and be satisfied): śābaʿ (to be satisfied, sated) is the covenant-blessing fullness — not mere survival but abundance. Deut 8:10-12 warns against forgetting YHWH precisely when śābaʿ is achieved.
11.16: hishamevu lachem pen-yiphteh levavchem (hišāmĕrû lākem pen-yipteh lĕbabkem): 'Watch yourselves lest your heart be deceived.' pātâ (to be deceived, enticed, opened wide): the same root as in 13:6 (the enticer). The heart-deception leads to turning aside to serve other gods — sārtem waʿăbadtem ʾĕlōhîm ʾăḥērîm. The self-warning (hišāmĕrû lākem) is the reflexive imperative: guard yourselves; the danger is internal spiritual seduction, not only external pressure.
11.17: vehara af YHWH bachem (wĕḥārâ ʾap̄ Yhwh bākem): 'YHWH's anger will burn against you.' ḥārâ ʾap̄ (the nose/nostril burned): the idiom for fierce divine anger — the nose as the organ of breath and emotion in Hebrew physiology. The covenant curse sequence follows: no rain, ground bears no produce, exile from the good land. The agricultural withdrawal is the visible sign of broken covenant — YHWH reverses the blessings of vv.13-15.
11.18: vesamtem et-devarai eleh al-levavchem (wĕśamtem ʾet-dĕbārāy ʾēlleh ʿal-lĕbabkem): 'Place these words of mine on your hearts.' śûm ʿal-lēb (to set upon the heart): the Hebrew idiom for internalization, taking something to heart. The subsequent actions — binding on the hand, between the eyes, teaching to children, writing on doorposts (mĕzûzôt) — externalize what is first internal. tôṭāp̄ôt (frontlets/phylacteries): the symbol between the eyes; later worn as physical tĕp̄illîn in Jewish practice. The placement sequence (heart → hand → forehead → doorpost → gate) moves from the most internal to the most public.
11.19: velimadtem otam et-beneihem (wĕlimmdtem ʾōtām ʾet-bĕnêkem): 'Teach them to your children.' limmēd (to teach, train): the piel intensive of lāmad (to learn). The Torah's transmission is communal and generational — the command-love of 6:6-9 is repeated here in identical language, emphasizing the pedagogical responsibility of parents. dabbēr bām (speak them): in every life-setting — sitting, walking, lying down, rising up — Torah saturates daily existence.
11.20: ukhtavtam al-mezuzot beitecha (ûkĕtabtām ʿal-mĕzûzôt bêtekā): 'Write them on the doorposts of your house.' mĕzûzâ (doorpost): the vertical wooden post at a door's side; the practice of affixing a small scroll (mĕzûzâ in later usage) to doorposts derives from this command. The gate (šaʿar) extends the Torah-marking to public space. The household and its public entry point are both claimed by the covenant word.
11.21: leyan yirbu yemeichem (lĕmaʿan yirbû yĕmêkem): 'That your days may be multiplied.' The lĕmaʿan (in order that) teleological formula — the covenant's purpose is life and length of days in the promised land. yimê haššāmayim ʿal-hāʾāreṣ (the days of heaven upon the earth): a beautiful hyperbole for indefinite duration — as long as sky is above earth. The land-tenure formula pairs with obedience; disobedience forfeits the promise.
11.22: ki im-shamor tishmeroon (kî ʾim-šāmōr tišmĕrûn): 'If you carefully observe.' Again the double infinitive absolute intensifier (šāmōr tišmĕrûn). dābaq (to hold fast, cleave): the covenantal-adhesion term — used of husband and wife in Gen 2:24, of Ruth and Naomi in Ruth 1:14, and here of Israel's obligation to YHWH. dābaq is the most intimate of the covenant-loyalty verbs, denoting personal attachment rather than mere compliance.
11.23: vehorish YHWH et-kol-hagoyim: 'YHWH will drive out all the nations.' yāraš (to dispossess, take possession): the key land-theology verb — both the dispossessing of the Canaanites and the possessing by Israel are expressed by the same root. The nations are described as 'greater and mightier than you' — the dispossession is explicitly beyond Israel's own military capacity, requiring divine action.
11.24: kol-hamakom asher tidroch kaf-raglechem bo lachem yihyeh: 'Every place where you set the sole of your foot shall be yours.' The land grant is expressed in the bodily imagery of foot-on-ground — the physical act of walking the territory constitutes possession in ancient Near Eastern legal custom. The promised boundaries: wilderness to Lebanon, Euphrates to Western Sea (hayyām hāʾaḥărôn, the Mediterranean).
11.25: pachdam vemoraam (paḥdĕkem ûmôrāʾăkem): 'Your terror and dread.' paḥad (terror, dread) and môrāʾ (fear, reverential dread): the two fear-words denote the existential dread YHWH will place in the nations' hearts at Israel's approach. This reverses the fear-formula: Israel need not fear (v.25a: 'no man shall stand against you') because YHWH instills fear in the nations. The holy-war theology of Deut is grounded in divine action, not Israelite strength.
11.26: reeh anochi noten lifneichem hayom (rĕʾēh ʾānōkî nōtēn lip̄nêkem hayyôm): 'See, I am placing before you today.' rĕʾēh (see!) — the singular imperative of perception, calling each individual to direct moral attention. berākâ ûqĕlālâ (blessing and curse): the covenant's binary structure that governs Deut 28-30. The 'today' (hayyôm) is the existential now of covenant decision — the choice is always present and urgent.
11.27: et-haberacha asher tishmeʿu: 'The blessing, if you obey.' The blessing is conditional on šāmaʿ (hearing/obeying) — 'if you obey the commandments of YHWH your God that I am commanding you today.' The Hebrew conditional structure: ʾăšer tišmĕʿû... hammitsvâ... ʾăšer ʾānōkî mĕṣawweh ʾetkem hayyôm — the blessing is inextricably bound to obedience.
11.28: vehakelala im-lo tishmeʿu: 'The curse, if you do not obey.' The curse (qĕlālâ) is the consequence of not-hearing — lōʾ tišmĕʿû. sārtem min-hadderek (turning aside from the way): sûr (to turn aside, deviate) is the standard verb for apostasy in Deuteronomy — leaving the covenant path. The 'other gods' you have not known: ʾĕlōhîm ʾăḥērîm ʾăšer lōʾ-yĕdaʿtem — the gods without covenant history with Israel.
11.29: venatata et-haberacha al-har gerizim: 'Set the blessing on Mount Gerizim, the curse on Mount Ebal.' The two mountains of the Shechem valley — the geography of covenant ratification (Josh 8:30-35 enacts Deut 27-28). Gerizim (traditionally the blessing-mountain, to the south; the Samaritan temple stood here) and ʿEbal (the curse-mountain, to the north). The landscape itself becomes the covenant's visible declaration.
11.30: halo-hema beeiver haYarden: 'Are they not beyond the Jordan?' The geographical orientation — 'beyond the Jordan, toward the setting sun, in the land of the Canaanites in the Arabah, opposite Gilgal, beside the oaks of Moreh' — grounds the covenant ceremony in a specific, visitable landscape. The precision of the geographical description serves the covenant's historical particularity: this is not mythology but history attached to known places.
11.31: ki atem ovrim et-haYarden: 'For you are crossing the Jordan.' The crossing (ʿābar) is the liminal moment — the covenant people on the threshold of the promised land. The entire book of Deuteronomy is Moses's final address before the crossing; the covenant renewal here is the preparation for what follows in Joshua. The imperative to obey 'all the statutes and ordinances' applies to the land they are entering to possess.
11.32: ushmartem laasot et-kol-hachuqqim: 'Keep and do all the statutes and ordinances.' The closing verse of the chapter pairs šāmar (keep, guard) with ʿāśâ (do, perform) — both verbs required: internalized guarding AND external performance. The land they are about to enter demands both dimensions of covenant loyalty.

12.1: eleh hachuqqim vehamishpatim (ʾēlleh haḥuqqîm wĕhammišpāṭîm): 'These are the statutes and ordinances.' Chapter 12 opens the Deuteronomic Law Code (chs 12-26) — the specific legal applications of the broad covenant command of chs 1-11. ḥuqqîm (statutes, fixed rules) and mišpāṭîm (ordinances, case-law decisions): the two principal legal categories. The land is the context — 'in the land which YHWH the God of your fathers has given you to possess, all the days that you live on the earth.'
12.2: abbed tabdun et-kol-hamqomot (ʾabbēd tĕʾabbĕdûn ʾet-kol-hammĕqōmôt): 'Completely destroy all the places.' Again the intensive double construction (ʾabbēd tĕʾabbĕdûn — double piel of ʾābad, to destroy). mĕqōmôt (places): the high places, hillsides, and trees where the Canaanite gods were worshipped. The command is not merely to stop using them but to eliminate them — ʿal-hehārîm hārāmîm wĕʿal-haggĕbāʿôt wĕtaḥat kol-ʿēṣ raʿănān (on the high mountains, on the hills, under every green tree).
12.3: nittazttem et-mizbehotam: 'Tear down their altars.' The four verbs of cultic destruction: nāttaṣ (tear down), šābar (smash), śārap̄ (burn), kārat (cut down). Each object: mizbĕḥôt (altars), maṣṣēbôt (sacred pillars, standing stones), ʾăšērîm (Asherah poles, wooden cult objects), and pĕsîlîm (carved/graven images). The divine name (šēm) of the Canaanite deities is to be destroyed — the name represents the deity's presence and power.
12.4: lo-taaʿsun ken laYHWH eloheichem: 'You must not do so to YHWH your God.' The explicit prohibition of Canaanite worship-practices applied to YHWH — YHWH is not to be worshipped in the same manner as the Canaanite gods. The verse is cryptic but decisive: whatever the Canaanites did at their scattered high places is forbidden for YHWH's worship. The positive alternative follows in v.5: YHWH chooses a single place.
12.5: ki im el-hamaqom asher-yivchar YHWH eloheichem (kî ʾim-ʾel-hammāqôm ʾăšer-yibḥar Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkem): 'Only to the place that YHWH your God chooses.' The theological heart of Deuteronomy 12: the centralization formula. yibḥar (he will choose): the divine election of the sanctuary site is YHWH's prerogative, not Israel's. lĕšakkēn šĕmô šām (to make his name dwell there): the Name-theology of Deuteronomy — YHWH does not corporally dwell in the temple (contrast Solomon's prayer, 1 Kings 8:27: 'even heaven cannot contain you') but places his šēm (name, representative presence) there. This nuance safeguards divine transcendence while asserting real divine presence.
12.6: uveatem shama oloteichem: 'There you shall bring your burnt offerings.' The list of sacrificial types required at the central sanctuary: ʿōlôt (burnt offerings), zeḇāḥîm (sacrifices), maʿăśrôt (tithes), tĕrûmat yēdkem (heave offerings), nĕdārîm (vow offerings), nĕdābôt (freewill offerings), and the firstborn of flocks and herds. The comprehensive catalog emphasizes that all worship-transactions with YHWH require the central place.
12.7: vaachaltem sham lifnei YHWH eloheichem: 'There you shall eat before YHWH your God.' The covenant meal eaten 'before YHWH' (lip̄nê Yhwh) in the sanctuary is not merely a meal but a communion act — Israel eating in the divine presence. ûśĕmaḥtem (rejoice): the joy-command that runs through Deuteronomy's festival legislation (14:26; 16:11,14; 26:11). The centralized sanctuary is not an onerous restriction but the place of covenant celebration and abundance.
12.8: lo taaʿsun kekol asher anachnu osim po hayom: 'You shall not do as we are doing here today.' The ʾîš kol-hayyāšār bĕʿênāyw (each man doing what is right in his own eyes) formula — the antithesis of the centralized covenant order. The chaotic worship of the wilderness period (each according to his own judgment) is contrasted with the ordered central-sanctuary worship that the entry into the land will establish. Judges 17:6; 21:25 use the same phrase for the moral chaos of the settlement period.
12.9: ki lo batem ad-ata el-hamenucha veEl-hanachala: 'You have not yet come to the rest and the inheritance.' mĕnûḥâ (rest, resting-place): the theological rest that the land represents — the cessation of the wilderness wandering. naḥălâ (inheritance): the land as divine gift-inheritance, not merely a territory won by military conquest. The double term anticipates the settled, stable worship at the chosen place — rest and inheritance are prerequisites for the centralized sanctuary order.
12.10: vaavartm et-haYarden vishavtem baareẓ: 'When you cross the Jordan and dwell in the land.' The conditional structure of Deut 12:1-28 — the laws apply fully only when the land is possessed and rest is achieved. wĕhēnîaḥ lākem mibbāl ʾōyĕbêkem (when he gives you rest from all your enemies): the military rest is the precondition for the cultic order. Hebrews 4:1-11 uses the mĕnûḥâ concept as a type for the final eschatological rest in Christ.
12.11: vehaya hamaqom asher-yivchar YHWH eloheichem bo: 'It shall be the place YHWH your God chooses for his name to dwell.' The centralization formula repeated (vv.5,11,14,18,21,26) — its ninefold repetition in ch 12 marks it as the theological spine of the entire legal corpus. The accumulated weight of 'the place that YHWH chooses' drives home the principle: divine election, not human convenience, determines the locus of worship.
12.12: usmachtem lifnei YHWH eloheichem: 'Rejoice before YHWH your God.' śāmaḥ (to rejoice) is a covenant-obligation as much as a response. The inclusion of sons, daughters, male and female servants, and the Levite within your gates — the total household including dependents — in the sanctuary celebration is Deuteronomy's consistent social vision: covenant joy is communal and inclusive.
12.13: hishamer lecha pen-taaleh oloteicha: 'Take care not to offer your burnt offerings at every place.' The negative form of the centralization command — explicit prohibition of sacrifice at any location chosen by the worshipper. hišāmer (take care, be careful): the reflexive warning. The positive follows: 'only in the place that YHWH chooses in one of your tribes.'
12.14: ki im-bamaqom asher-yivchar YHWH: 'Only in the place that YHWH chooses in one of your tribes.' The indefiniteness of 'one of your tribes' is deliberate — the specific place (Jerusalem/Zion) is not named in Deuteronomy. The principle of divine election precedes the specific location; the people commit to following YHWH's choice before the choice is made known.
12.15: raq bechol-avat nafshecha tizbach veachalta vasar: 'Only you may slaughter and eat meat within your towns whenever you desire.' The concession for secular slaughter (mĕhôl) outside the sanctuary — meat may be eaten for sustenance without sacrificial ritual, unlike the earlier system (Lev 17:1-7 required all slaughter to be sacrificial). Deuteronomy permits what is called šĕḥîṭat ḥullîn (secular/non-sacred slaughter) — a practical concession for the increased distance from the central sanctuary. The clean and unclean together may eat: haṭṭāhôr wĕhaṭṭāmēʾ — without the purity constraints of sacrificial eating.
12.16: raq hadam lo tochal: 'Only you shall not eat the blood.' dām (blood): the prohibition of blood-consumption is foundational (Lev 17:10-14; Gen 9:4; Acts 15:29). The blood belongs to YHWH as the vehicle of life — ʿal-hāʾāreṣ tišpĕkennû kammāyim (pour it on the ground like water): the blood is returned to the earth rather than consumed, acknowledging that the life belongs to God.
12.17: lo tuchal leechol bisheareicha maʿsar: 'You may not eat within your towns the tithe of your grain, wine, and oil.' The distinction between ordinary sustenance (permitted locally, v.15) and the covenant-sacred items (tithes, firstborn, vow offerings) that must be eaten at the central sanctuary. The list of items requiring pilgrimage to the central place: maʿăśar dĕgānĕkā wĕtîrōšĕkā wĕyiṣhārekā (tithes of grain, new wine, oil), firstborn of herd and flock, vow offerings, freewill offerings, heave offerings.
12.18: ki im-lifnei YHWH eloheicha tocharlennu: 'Only before YHWH your God shall you eat it.' The items withheld from local consumption must be consumed 'before YHWH' — in his presence, at the central sanctuary, in a communal celebration that includes the household and the Levite. The joy-command returns: ûśāmaḥtā lip̄nê Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā — the centralization's purpose is not restriction but celebration in the divine presence.
12.19: hishamer lecha pen-taazov et-halevi: 'Take care not to neglect the Levite.' The Levite has no territorial inheritance (ḥēleq wĕnaḥălâ) — his portion is YHWH himself (10:9; 18:1-2). The repeated concern for the Levite within Israel's towns across Deuteronomy (12:12,18,19; 14:27,29; 16:11,14; 26:12-13) reflects the social realities of cultic centralization: Levites who served at local shrines needed sustained community support once those shrines were abolished.
12.20: ki-yarchiv YHWH eloheicha et-gevulcha: 'When YHWH your God extends your territory.' The provision for secular meat-eating is made in anticipation of the land's expansion, when distance from the central sanctuary makes pilgrimage for every meat-meal impractical. weʾāmartā ʾōklâ bāśār (you say 'I will eat meat'): the natural human desire for meat is legitimate; YHWH's provision is practical and human-scale.
12.21: veHAYA im-yirchaq mimcha haMaqom asher yivchar YHWH: 'If the place that YHWH chooses is far from you.' Distance is the practical consideration that triggers the secular-slaughter permission. wĕzābaḥtā mibbĕqārĕkā ûmiṣṣōʾnĕkā (slaughter from your herd and flock) 'as I have commanded you' — even secular slaughter has a prescribed manner (ʾăšer ṣiwwîtîkā). The humane slaughter laws implicit in this phrase became the basis for rabbinic šĕḥîṭâ regulations.
12.22: ach kaaher yochal et-hatzvi veEt-haAYAL: 'Just as the gazelle and the deer are eaten.' The clean wild animals (whose blood is not brought to the altar and poured against it) become the model for secular slaughter — neither the blood-sprinkling ritual nor the fat-burning applies. The democratization of meat-eating that Deuteronomy permits is a significant departure from the Leviticus 17 system, reflecting the changed circumstances of settled land life far from the central sanctuary.
12.23: raq chazaq levilti achal hadam (raq ḥăzaq lĕbiltî ʾăkōl haddām): 'Only be strong in not eating the blood.' ḥăzaq (be strong, be firm): the blood prohibition requires moral strength and resolution to maintain. The theological rationale: kî haddām hûʾ hannep̄eš (for the blood is the life/soul). nepeš (life, soul, being): the blood as the carrier of the nepeš (the animating life-force) is the most fundamental principle of Israelite food law. Lev 17:11 adds: 'the life of the flesh is in the blood; I have given it to you on the altar to make atonement for your souls.'
12.24: lo tochlenu: 'Do not eat it.' The repeated blood prohibition (vv.16,23,24,25) through intensification. ʿal-hāʾāreṣ tišpĕkennû kammāyim (pour it on the ground like water): the blood returned to the earth reverses the sacrificial blood that is collected and dashed against the altar. Non-sacrificial slaughter still requires the blood's return — the life belongs to YHWH even when the killing is secular.
12.25: lo tochlenu lemaʿan yitav lecha: 'Do not eat it, that it may go well with you.' The motivational formula: obedience to the blood law produces well-being (ṭôb) for the individual and descendants. The covenant-reward structure: right practice → divine blessing. The blood law is not arbitrary but carries the weight of the covenant's own survival logic — the life belongs to the giver of life.
12.26: raq qodasheicha asher-yihyu lecha: 'But your holy things and your vow offerings.' The return to the central-sanctuary requirement for the sacred items — vowed sacrifices (nĕdārîm) must be brought to the chosen place. The distinction sacred/secular (consecrated offering vs. ordinary meat) determines where the eating occurs.
12.27: veAsita olotecha habasar vehadam: 'Offer your burnt offerings, the flesh and the blood, on the altar.' The burnt offering is the total offering — bāśār wĕdām (flesh and blood) both belong to YHWH, consumed entirely. The peace offering (zeḇaḥ šĕlāmîm) differs: the blood is dashed against the altar, the fat burned, but the flesh eaten by the worshipper 'before YHWH.' The two-way flow of the communion meal: YHWH receives the blood and fat, Israel eats the flesh.
12.28: shemor veshimaʿta et-kol-hadevarim haeleh: 'Observe and obey all these words.' The summary command closing the central-sanctuary legislation. lĕmaʿan yîṭab lĕkā ûlĕbānêkā (that it may go well for you and your children): the covenant-benefit formula applied to the entire legal corpus of ch 12. The chapter ends with the same well-being motivation as v.25 — the laws are for Israel's flourishing, not arbitrary restriction.
12.29: ki-yachrit YHWH eloheicha et-hagoyim: 'When YHWH your God cuts off the nations before you.' The dispossession formula opens the warning against Canaanite worship-imitation. pen-tinaqesh achareihem (lest you be ensnared by following them): nāqaš (to be ensnared, trapped): the Canaanite practices as a trap. The theological danger: Israel, having displaced the Canaanites, might be attracted to the cultic practices that 'worked' in the land.
12.30: pen-tidrosh leEloheihem leymor: 'Lest you inquire about their gods, saying.' The curiosity-trap — not active worship but theological inquiry into Canaanite religion. êkâ yaʿabdû haggôyîm hāʾēlleh ʾet-ʾĕlōhêhem (how did these nations serve their gods?): the inquiry that leads to syncretism. The imitative impulse is the gateway to apostasy — the Deuteronomist understands the psychological seduction of religious curiosity.
12.31: lo-taaʿseh ken lYHWH eloheicha: 'You shall not do so to YHWH your God.' The Canaanite worship practices are described as abominations (tôʿēbat Yhwh) — including child sacrifice (ki gam ʾet-bĕnêhem wĕʾet-bĕnōtêhem yiśrĕpû bāʾēš lēʾlōhêhem, 'for they even burned their sons and daughters in the fire for their gods'). The Molech cult is the extreme point of Canaanite religious practice — the total sacrifice of children to secure divine favor.
12.32: et-kol-haDavar asher anochi metzaveh etchem oto tishmerunu laasot: 'Everything I command you, be careful to do it.' The closing formula of chapter 12: neither add nor subtract (lōʾ tōsēp̄ ʿālāyw wĕlōʾ tigdaʿ mimmennû). The completeness and sufficiency of the Mosaic Torah — no human additions or subtractions permitted. Deut 4:2 opens with the same formula; the book is bracketed by the sufficiency of the divine word.

13.1: ki-yaqum beqirBEKHA navi o ḥolam ḥalom (kî-yāqûm bĕqirbĕkā nābîʾ ʾô ḥōlēm ḥălôm): 'If a prophet or a dreamer of dreams arises among you.' Chapter 13 deals with three scenarios of enticement to apostasy. nābîʾ (prophet) and ḥōlēm ḥălôm (dreamer of dreams): the two recognized channels of divine communication in Israel's prophetic tradition. The test case is deliberately extreme — what if the sign actually comes to pass?
13.2: venatan eleicha ot o mofet (wĕnātan ʾēlêkā ʾôt ʾô mōp̄ēt): 'And the sign or wonder comes to pass.' ʾôt (sign) and mōp̄ēt (wonder, portent): the same sign-wonder pair from the exodus (4:34; 6:22; 7:19; 26:8) — but here fulfilled by a false prophet. The scenario is shocking: even verified predictive ability does not authenticate the prophet if he directs Israel toward other gods. Accuracy is necessary but not sufficient for prophetic authority — the content determines the verdict.
13.3: lo tishmaʿ el-divrei hanavi hahu (lōʾ tišmaʿ ʾel-dibrê hannābîʾ hahûʾ): 'Do not listen to the words of that prophet.' The command is absolute — regardless of the sign's fulfillment. ki menasseh YHWH eloheichem etchem (for YHWH your God is testing you): the false prophet who produces genuine signs is permitted by YHWH as a nissāyôn (test, trial) — to reveal whether Israel's love for YHWH is absolute or contingent on miraculous display. Matt 24:24 and Rev 13:13-14 use the same logic: false prophets will produce great signs to deceive.
13.4: acharei YHWH eloheichem telchu: 'Follow the LORD your God.' The five verbs of total covenant loyalty: hālak ʾaḥărê (follow after), yārēʾ (fear), šāmar miṣwōt (keep commandments), šāmaʿ bĕqōlô (obey his voice), ʿābaḏ (serve), dābaq (hold fast). The five-fold response is the proper orientation that the false prophet's enticement tests. The test reveals whether this comprehensive loyalty is genuine or merely formal.
13.5: veHAnavi hahu o ḥolam haḥalom hahu yumat (wĕhannābîʾ hahûʾ ʾô ḥōlēm haḥălôm hahûʾ yûmāt): 'That prophet or dreamer shall be put to death.' The death penalty for the false prophet — regardless of whether his signs came true. kî dibber-sārâ ʿal-Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkem (for he has spoken rebellion against YHWH your God): sārâ (rebellion, apostasy) — the technical term for the covenant treason that warrants the most severe penalty. ûbiʿartā hārāʿ miqqirbĕkā (purge the evil from your midst): the biʿēr (purge/burn out) formula that runs through Deuteronomy's penal code (17:7,12; 19:13,19; 21:21; 22:21-24; 24:7).
13.6: ki yesitcha acheicha ben-immecha (kî yĕsîtĕkā ʾāḥîkā ben-ʾimmekā): 'If your brother, the son of your mother, entices you.' The second scenario: the intimate enticer — family member (brother, son, daughter, wife, close friend). mēsît (enticer): from sût (to incite, persuade, entice) — the same root used for Satan enticing David (1 Chr 21:1) and YHWH testing Job (Job 2:3). The social proximity makes the enticement most dangerous — it comes wrapped in personal loyalty and love.
13.7: nelcha venavda elohim aḥerim: “Let us go and serve other gods.” The content of the enticement: gods of the surrounding peoples — mēʾĕlōhê hāʿammîm ʾăšer sĕbîbōtêkem (from the gods of the peoples surrounding you), near or far, unknown. The anonymity of the suggested gods ('which neither you nor your fathers have known') emphasizes their lack of covenant history — no basis for loyalty except the enticer's relationship with you.
13.8: lo-tove lo velo tishmaʿ elav: 'Do not yield to him or listen to him.' The four prohibitions toward the intimate enticer: lōʾ-ʾābeh (do not yield), lōʾ-tišmaʿ (do not listen), lōʾ-tāḥôs ʿênĕkā (do not have pity), lōʾ tĕḥuml (do not show compassion), lōʾ tĕkassē ʿālāyw (do not conceal him). The cascade of negatives describes the total severance of relationship loyalty where covenant loyalty is at stake. The law is radical and deliberately shocking — it requires placing YHWH above even the most intimate human bonds.
13.9: ki harog tahargennu (kî hārōg tahargennû): 'You shall surely kill him.' The intensified double construction (hārōg tahargennû — infinitive absolute + finite verb) demands the death penalty. yādĕkā tihyeh bô bāriʾšōnâ lĕhāmîtô (your hand shall be first against him to put him to death): the accuser executes first. The public execution and the subsequent lapidation (rāgam bāʾăbānîm) witnesses the community's covenant solidarity.
13.10: usekaltho baavanim vaMET: 'Stone him to death.' ûsĕqaltô bāʾăbānîm wāmēt: the stoning is the community's collective act — the mob-execution that distributes responsibility across the whole body. kî biqqēš lĕhadd ōtĕkā mēʿal Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā (for he sought to drive you from YHWH your God): niddaḥ (to drive away, banish): the enticement's goal is covenant expulsion — separation from the relationship that alone gives life.
13.11: vechal-Yisrael yishmeʿu veyireʾu: 'All Israel shall hear and fear.' yišmĕʿû wĕyîrĕʾû (shall hear and fear): the public execution's deterrent function. The execution's publicity transforms individual punishment into communal instruction. wĕlōʾ-yōsîp̄û laʿăśôt kaddābār hārāʿ hazzeh bĕqirbĕkā (shall no longer do such evil in your midst): the deterrence logic of Deuteronomy's penal code — the punishment prevents the replication of the crime.
13.12: ki-tishmaʿ beachat areicah asher YHWH eloheicha noten lecha: 'If you hear in one of your cities that YHWH is giving you.' The third scenario: the devoted city (ʿîr hanniddaḥat).
